AI.motivation: Existing symbolic benchmarks are limited to mathematical reasoning, lack visual grounding, and are predominantly English-only. The authors aimed to create a more comprehensive benchmark to assess model robustness under minor modifications across STEM disciplines and languages.
AI.method: Domain experts (including Olympiad medalists) crafted 4,242 problem templates (606 per language) across 5 STEM subjects and 7 languages. Templates are executable Python code generating diverse instances by varying numerical values, visual patterns, geometric shapes, color schemes, and function types. 17 state-of-the-art VLMs were evaluated.
AI.result: Found a noticeable gap between worst-case accuracy (proportion of templates answered correctly across all variations) and average accuracy. Smaller models show performance degradation across languages while proprietary/larger models remain robust. Step-level evaluation shows similar gap between average F1 and worst-case F1. Attention head analysis reveals cross-lingual variation in relative attention to image vs. text tokens.
AI.conclusion: The work highlights the importance of evaluation beyond static benchmarks as a metric to measure the quality of VLMs.

title: DyCo-RL: Dynamic Cross-Modal Coordination for Visual Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a leading paradigm for enhancing visual reasoning in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). However, existing RLVR methods optimize primarily for the reasoning outcome, fundamentally overlooking the fine-grained cross-modal coordination required during the generation process. Through token-level analyses and controlled interventions, we reveal that during Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning, MLLMs frequently fail to dynamically alternate between extracting visual evidence and synthesizing textual context-a coordination breakdown that is causally linked to reasoning failures. Motivated by these findings, we propose DyCo-RL, which integrates dynamic cross-modal coordination into RLVR optimization. Specifically, DyCo-RL uses the Fisher-Rao geodesic distance to measure within-modality attention shifts, assigning tokens to either visually-oriented or text-oriented functional roles. It then evaluates the alignment between a token's actual attention allocation and its assigned role, leveraging this score for alignment-guided advantage reweighting during policy optimization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the algorithm-agnostic DyCo-RL, when applied to Qwen2.5-VL-3B/7B, consistently improves four representative RLVR algorithms across seven benchmarks spanning visual-centric and mathematical reasoning.

AI.tldr: DyCo-RL improves visual reasoning in MLLMs by integrating dynamic cross-modal coordination into RLVR optimization, using Fisher-Rao geodesic distance to measure attention shifts and alignment-guided advantage reweighting, consistently boosting four RLVR algorithms across seven benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing RLVR methods only optimize for reasoning outcomes, ignoring the fine-grained cross-modal coordination (alternating between visual evidence extraction and textual context synthesis) during the CoT generation process, which is causally linked to reasoning failures.
AI.method: DyCo-RL uses Fisher-Rao geodesic distance to measure within-modality attention shifts, assigns tokens to visually-oriented or text-oriented roles, evaluates token-level attention-role alignment, and uses this score for alignment-guided advantage reweighting during policy optimization.
AI.result: Applied to Qwen2.5-VL-3B/7B, DyCo-RL consistently improves four representative RLVR algorithms across seven benchmarks spanning visual-centric and mathematical reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: Addressing cross-modal coordination at the token level during RLVR optimization significantly enhances visual reasoning in MLLMs, and the approach is algorithm-agnostic, generalizing across different RLVR methods.

title: Robust-U1: Can MLLMs Self-Recover Corrupted Visual Content for Robust Understanding?
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated remarkable success in visual understanding, yet their performance degrades significantly under real-world visual corruptions. While existing robustness enhancement approaches exist, they are limited: black-box feature alignment lacks interpretability, and white-box text-based reasoning cannot restore lost pixel-level details. This work investigates a fundamental research question: Can MLLMs recover corrupted visual content by themselves? To address this, we propose Robust-U1, a novel framework that equips MLLMs with explicit visual self-recovery capability for robust understanding. The approach comprises three core stages: supervised fine-tuning for initial reconstruction, reinforcement learning with dual rewards (pixel-level SSIM and semantic-level CLIP similarity) for aligning high visual quality, and multimodal reasoning that jointly considers both the corrupted input and the recovered image. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Robust-U1 achieves state-of-the-art robustness on the real-world corruption benchmark and maintains superior performance under adversarial corruptions on general VQA benchmarks. Analysis confirms that high-quality visual recovery directly enhances reasoning performance, establishing self-recovery as a critical mechanism for robust visual understanding. The source code is available at https://github.com/jqtangust/Robust-U1.

AI.tldr: Robust-U1 is a framework that equips Multimodal Large Language Models with explicit visual self-recovery capability to handle real-world and adversarial visual corruptions, using supervised fine-tuning, reinforcement learning with dual rewards, and multimodal reasoning.
AI.motivation: MLLMs degrade significantly under real-world visual corruptions, and existing robustness methods are limited—black-box alignment lacks interpretability while white-box reasoning cannot restore lost pixel-level details.
AI.method: Three-stage framework: (1) supervised fine-tuning for initial reconstruction, (2) reinforcement learning with dual rewards (pixel-level SSIM + semantic-level CLIP similarity) for high-quality visual alignment, (3) multimodal reasoning jointly using corrupted input and recovered image.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art robustness on real-world corruption benchmarks and maintains superior performance under adversarial corruptions on general VQA benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: High-quality visual recovery directly enhances reasoning performance, establishing self-recovery as a critical mechanism for robust visual understanding in MLLMs.

title: VideoWeaver: Evaluating and Evolving Skills for Agentic Long Video Generation
summary: Recent agent frameworks such as Claude Code, Codex, and OpenClaw are strong at tool use and orchestration, but whether they can handle long video generation, a long-horizon multimodal task, remains underexplored. Unlike earlier video agents whose pipeline is handcrafted, these frameworks can build and refine their own workflows. We introduce VideoWeaver, an agent harness and benchmark that evaluates and evolves skills for long video generation, where an agent turns a single instruction into a long video by composing foundation skills into its own workflow rather than following a predefined pipeline. The benchmark has 16 task categories and 285 cases, with references spanning text, image, audio, video, and their combinations. Because errors can arise at any stage and not just in the final video, we propose an agent-as-judge that inspects both the execution trace and the final video, grounding its scores in evidence such as metadata and intermediate files. Using this feedback, we further design a skill evolution algorithm that refines and merges the agent's skills. Across multiple frameworks and models, we find that an explicit composition skill improves the generation process over using foundation skills alone, that skill evolution further improves output quality, and that performance varies notably across harness and model choices. The proposed agent-as-judge also aligns well with human judgments, especially on process metrics. Code and dataset is available at https://github.com/JianhuiWei7/VideoWeaver

AI.tldr: VideoWeaver is a benchmark and agent harness for long video generation that lets agents compose foundation skills into custom workflows, features 285 test cases across 16 categories, uses an agent-as-judge for evaluating both process and output, and includes a skill evolution algorithm. Results show explicit composition skills outperform foundation skills alone, and the judge aligns well with human evaluation.
AI.motivation: Existing agent frameworks excel at tool use but are underexplored for long-horizon multimodal tasks like long video generation. Previous video agents rely on handcrafted pipelines, lacking the ability to build and refine their own workflows adaptively.
AI.method: VideoWeaver provides a harness and benchmark with 16 task categories and 285 cases. It introduces an agent-as-judge that inspects both execution trace and final video using metadata and intermediate files. A skill evolution algorithm refines and merges agent skills based on this feedback.
AI.result: Explicit composition skills improve generation over foundation skills alone; skill evolution further enhances output quality; performance varies across harness and model choices; the agent-as-judge aligns well with human judgments, especially on process metrics.
AI.conclusion: Agent frameworks with self-composed workflows outperform fixed pipelines for long video generation. The agent-as-judge and skill evolution are effective. Open-source code and dataset are released for further research.

title: Trustworthy Visual Predicates for Robust Manipulation Understanding under Degradation
summary: Manipulation understanding requires reliable relational evidence, such as contact, support, containment, motion coupling, grasp, release, and active-hand involvement. Although these visual predicates are widely used in event-chain, graph-based, and neuro-symbolic models, their reliability under visual degradation is rarely analyzed directly. This paper introduces a predicate-level reliability framework for robust manipulation understanding under blur, occlusion, illumination change, low resolution, frame dropping, and detection noise. The framework defines a structured predicate vocabulary, confidence-aware predicate estimation, and reliability metrics for predicate preservation, degradation sensitivity, temporal consistency, confidence-weighted stability, and downstream impact. Experiments on controlled manipulation videos and public egocentric or bimanual datasets, including VISOR/EPIC-KITCHENS, H2O, and ARCTIC, show that predicate failures are structured rather than uniform. Static spatial predicates remain comparatively robust, whereas contact-sensitive, dynamic, and derived predicates such as grasp and release are more fragile. Under severe degradation, detection noise, occlusion, and frame dropping cause the strongest reliability losses. Downstream analysis shows that degraded predicates reduce manipulation-understanding accuracy from 0.89 to 0.58, while removing confidence weighting under moderate degradation reduces accuracy from 0.74 to 0.64. These results show that predicate reliability provides a diagnostic layer between visual perception and structured manipulation reasoning.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a predicate-level reliability framework for manipulation understanding under visual degradation (blur, occlusion, illumination change, low resolution, frame dropping, detection noise). The framework defines a structured predicate vocabulary with confidence-aware estimation and multiple reliability metrics. Experiments show that predicate failures are structured: static spatial predicates are robust, while contact-sensitive and dynamic predicates (grasp, release) are fragile. Degradation reduces manipulation accuracy from 0.89 to 0.58.
AI.motivation: Although visual predicates (contact, support, containment, grasp, release, etc.) are widely used in event-chain, graph-based, and neuro-symbolic models for manipulation understanding, their reliability under visual degradation has rarely been analyzed directly.
AI.method: The paper proposes a predicate-level reliability framework with: (1) structured predicate vocabulary, (2) confidence-aware predicate estimation, and (3) reliability metrics including predicate preservation, degradation sensitivity, temporal consistency, confidence-weighted stability, and downstream impact.
AI.result: Static spatial predicates remain robust, while contact-sensitive, dynamic, and derived predicates (grasp, release) are fragile. Detection noise, occlusion, and frame dropping cause strongest reliability losses. Degraded predicates reduce manipulation accuracy from 0.89 to 0.58; removing confidence weighting under moderate degradation reduces accuracy from 0.74 to 0.64.
AI.conclusion: Predicate reliability provides a diagnostic layer between visual perception and structured manipulation reasoning, showing that failures are structured rather than uniform.

title: Human-Centered Benchmarking of Driver Monitoring Models
comment: 9 pages, 3 figures, 7 tables. Code available at: https://github.com/rubendflorezzela/hcbf-driver-monitoring
summary: Vision-based driver monitoring systems are increasingly deployed in safety-critical intelligent transportation settings, yet they are almost always compared on classification accuracy alone. This paper argues that accuracy is insufficient to characterize a model's fitness for real-world deployment, and proposes the Human-Centered Benchmarking Framework (HCBF), which evaluates models across four dimensions: accuracy, explainability, efficiency, and robustness. The framework is applied to four representative lightweight architectures, MobileNetV3, ShuffleNetV2, EfficientNet-B0, and DeiT-Tiny, on the MRL Eye Dataset for eye-state classification. While the models are nearly indistinguishable on clean-set accuracy, each leads in exactly one dimension, and all four lie on the Pareto frontier. A Human-Centered Score computed under three deployment-oriented weighting scenarios ranks ShuffleNetV2 first throughout. However, this aggregate winner retains less than half of its performance under sensor noise and fails by classifying closed eyes as open, whereas the transformer remains robust. These findings show that aggregate ranking can mask dimension-specific vulnerabilities that are operationally decisive, underscoring the value of multi-dimensional, human-centered evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Human-Centered Benchmarking Framework (HCBF) evaluating models across accuracy, explainability, efficiency, and robustness. Applied to four lightweight architectures on eye-state classification, ShuffleNetV2 ranks first overall but fails under sensor noise, while the transformer remains robust, showing aggregate rankings can mask critical vulnerabilities.
AI.motivation: Current driver monitoring systems are evaluated primarily on classification accuracy, which is insufficient for determining real-world deployment fitness. The paper argues for a multi-dimensional human-centered evaluation approach.
AI.method: Proposes HCBF evaluating models across four dimensions: accuracy, explainability, efficiency, and robustness. Applied to MobileNetV3, ShuffleNetV2, EfficientNet-B0, and DeiT-Tiny on the MRL Eye Dataset. A Human-Centered Score is computed under three deployment-oriented weighting scenarios.
AI.result: Models are nearly indistinguishable on clean accuracy but each leads in exactly one dimension, all on Pareto frontier. ShuffleNetV2 ranks first under all weighting scenarios but retains less than half its performance under sensor noise, misclassifying closed eyes as open, while the transformer remains robust.
AI.conclusion: Aggregate ranking can mask dimension-specific vulnerabilities that are operationally decisive, highlighting the need for multi-dimensional, human-centered evaluation in safety-critical applications.

title: One Stone, Three Birds: Self-adaptive Optimal Transport for Multi-VLM Selection, Adaptation, and Ensembling
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) enable visual recognition from semantic class descriptions, which makes them attractive when target annotations are scarce or unavailable. Most deployment pipelines, however, first choose a single VLM and then adapt that model to the unlabeled target set. This single-backbone paradigm hides a critical assumption: the selected VLM is already compatible with the target domain. In realistic cross-domain deployment, several general-purpose and domain-specialized VLMs may be plausible, yet no instance-level target labels are available to identify the reliable ones. Deployment therefore requires a coupled solution for model selection, target adaptation, and prediction integration. We revisit this problem from a system-level multi-VLM perspective. Our central observation is that the three decisions above depend on the same latent object: a trustworthy sample-class structure in the target set. Different VLMs may encode different transfer biases and produce conflicting predictions, but their outputs can still provide complementary evidence for estimating this structure. We propose One Stone, Three Birds, a training-free framework based on self-adaptive optimal transport. Given a pool of frozen candidate VLMs, OSTB estimates a consensus sample-to-class transport plan without updating VLM parameters. The learned transport structure is then reused for all deployment objectives: model selection is performed by ranking the combined semantic and visual reliability induced by the consensus plan; target adaptation is obtained by fitting transport-conditioned visual classifiers; and ensembling is implemented through reliability-aware probabilistic integration. Extensive experiments on natural-image, remote-sensing, and medical-pathology benchmarks show that OSTB improves model ranking, adaptation stability, and ensemble robustness under heterogeneous candidate pools.

AI.tldr: OSTB is a training-free framework that uses self-adaptive optimal transport to jointly solve model selection, target adaptation, and prediction ensembling for multiple VLMs in cross-domain deployment without target labels.
AI.motivation: Single-backbone VLM deployment assumes the chosen model is compatible with the target domain, which is unrealistic when multiple general-purpose and domain-specialized VLMs are plausible but no target labels exist to identify reliable ones.
AI.method: A training-free framework (OSTB) that estimates a consensus sample-to-class transport plan from multiple frozen VLMs via self-adaptive optimal transport, then reuses this structure for model selection (ranking reliability), target adaptation (transport-conditioned classifiers), and ensembling (reliability-aware probabilistic integration).
AI.result: Extensive experiments on natural-image, remote-sensing, and medical-pathology benchmarks show improvements in model ranking, adaptation stability, and ensemble robustness under heterogeneous candidate pools.
AI.conclusion: The three deployment decisions (model selection, adaptation, ensembling) share a common latent structure (sample-class relationships), and solving for it jointly via optimal transport is more effective than treating them independently.

title: Phase Marginalization for Patch-Grid Instability in Vision Transformers
summary: Vision Transformers operate on fixed patch grids, which can introduce phase-dependent instability for dense prediction: changing the patch partition can change the token evidence available to a pixel, especially near boundaries. We formalize patch-grid phase as a nuisance variable and propose Phase Marginalization, a post-hoc marginalization method that evaluates structured patch-grid phases, inverse-aligns dense outputs, and aggregates them in the original image coordinate system. The central variant, Uniform Phase Marginalization with K = 4, is training-free and improves over the canonical K = 1 baseline across measured segmentation, depth, and local matching settings. In a controlled Cityscapes experiment, Uniform Phase Marginalization provides a modest compute-matched advantage over generic shift-based four-forward test-time augmentation (TTA) (+0.31 mean Intersection-over-Union over the strongest tested generic row). A scaling study further shows that K = 4 is a practical cost-accuracy trade-off: K = 8 is essentially unchanged and K = 16 adds little accuracy at much higher latency. These results position patch-grid phase as a measurable nuisance variable and Phase Marginalization as a simple diagnostic and post-hoc marginalization baseline for dense ViT prediction.

AI.tldr: Vision Transformers suffer from phase-dependent instability due to fixed patch grids. The authors propose Phase Marginalization, a post-hoc method that aggregates predictions across multiple patch-grid phases (K=4) to improve dense prediction tasks like segmentation, depth, and matching, outperforming standard test-time augmentation.
AI.motivation: Vision Transformers use fixed patch grids, causing instability in dense predictions when the patch partition changes (phase-dependent boundary effects). The authors formalize patch-grid phase as a nuisance variable that should be marginalized.
AI.method: Phase Marginalization evaluates multiple structured patch-grid phases (K=4 as the central variant), inverse-aligns dense outputs, and aggregates them in the original image coordinate system. This is a training-free, post-hoc method.
AI.result: Uniform Phase Marginalization (K=4) outperforms the canonical K=1 baseline across segmentation, depth, and local matching. On Cityscapes, it achieves +0.31 mIoU over the strongest generic shift-based four-forward TTA. K=8 shows negligible change, K=16 adds little accuracy at much higher latency.
AI.conclusion: Patch-grid phase is a measurable nuisance variable in dense ViT prediction. Phase Marginalization serves as a simple, effective diagnostic and post-hoc marginalization baseline.

title: Gravity-guided Contact Dynamics Estimation from 3D Human Motions
summary: Ground contact forces acting on the human body, are crucial for biomechanics studies or sport performance analysis. Prior methods rely on force plates or pressure mats to collect ground contact dynamics, limiting their applicability to carefully controlled settings. A more scalable solution is to estimate the dynamics directly from motion capture data. Recent approaches only roughly estimate the ground contact dynamics from the vertical distance between the body and the ground plane, which cannot capture the complex pressure distribution of all contact points. To this end, we propose GraCE -- Gravity-guided Contact Dynamics Estimation, a novel full-body contact dynamics model for human motions using a realistic influence of body mass distribution and gravity. We use the human's center of gravity to estimate the ground contacts based on its relative distance to the human body. The applied force on each contact is estimated via the product of predicted contact probabilities and the total exterior force computed from the center of mass trajectory. We outperform related work on the GroundLink dataset for ground reaction force estimation, and on the MOYO dataset for detailed contact pressure prediction. The code is published upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: GraCE uses center of gravity and body mass distribution to estimate ground contact forces and pressure distribution from motion capture data, outperforming prior methods on two benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Ground contact forces are essential for biomechanics and sports analysis, but existing methods require force plates/pressure mats (limited to lab settings) or crude distance-based estimates that fail to capture complex pressure distributions. A scalable, accurate estimation method from motion data alone is needed.
AI.method: GraCE leverages the human center of gravity (CoG) and its relative distance to body segments for contact estimation. Contact forces are computed as the product of predicted contact probabilities and total exterior force derived from center of mass trajectory, guided by gravity and mass distribution.
AI.result: Outperforms related work on the GroundLink dataset (ground reaction force estimation) and the MOYO dataset (detailed contact pressure prediction).
AI.conclusion: Gravity-guided estimation with center-of-gravity reasoning provides a more accurate and scalable approach to contact dynamics estimation from motion capture, eliminating the need for specialized hardware.

title: IMAGINE: Adaptive Schema-Imagery Enhanced Composition for Composed Video Retrieval
summary: Composed Video Retrieval (CVR) is designed to retrieve a target video that matches a reference video modified by a modification text. While existing methods explore cross-modal correspondences, they often assume modified objects appear directly in videos. However, modification texts frequently describe concepts not explicitly presented but implicitly expressed through semantically related visual cues (e.g., "cake" implying "birthday party"). Current approaches typically rely on aligning explicit feature representations within the concrete space, neglecting critical latent associations. To address this, we propose an adaptIve scheMa-ImAGery enhanced composItional NEtwork (IMAGINE). Unlike standard explicit matching, IMAGINE materializes implicit semantics (termed schema imagery) via dynamic multimodal prototypes. These prototypes capture shared latent concepts to adaptively modulate visual features, effectively injecting implicit guidance into the retrieval process. By bridging the gap between explicit visual contents and implicit retrieval intentions, IMAGINE achieves state-of-the-art performance in both CVR and Composed Image Retrieval (CIR) across three widely used benchmarks.

AI.tldr: IMAGINE is a novel network for Composed Video Retrieval that handles implicit semantics (e.g., "cake" implying "birthday party") by using dynamic multimodal prototypes to capture latent concepts and modulate visual features, achieving state-of-the-art results on CVR and CIR benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing CVR methods rely on explicit feature alignment but fail when modification texts describe concepts not directly shown in videos (implicit semantics like "cake" → "birthday party"). The paper aims to bridge this gap between explicit visual content and implicit retrieval intentions.
AI.method: Proposes IMAGINE (adaptIve scheMa-ImAGery enhanced composItional NEtwork) that materializes implicit semantics (schema imagery) via dynamic multimodal prototypes. These prototypes capture shared latent concepts and adaptively modulate visual features, injecting implicit guidance into retrieval.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on both Composed Video Retrieval (CVR) and Composed Image Retrieval (CIR) tasks across three widely used benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Bridging explicit visual contents and implicit retrieval intentions via dynamic schema imagery prototypes is effective for compositional retrieval tasks, outperforming existing explicit matching approaches.

title: Property-Informed Diffusion-Based Text-to-Microstructure Generation
comment: Published in CVPR2026, Code is at: https://github.com/hongsong-wang/PropDiff-TMG
summary: Designing 3D metamaterial microstructures that meet the intended functions remains a major challenge, as it typically requires domain expertise, iterative simulations, and extensive manual tuning. Existing work on inverse design that automatically generates microstructures based on desired target properties often suffers from limited design diversity and faces challenges in ensuring the physical feasibility of the generated structures. To address this issue, a property-informed diffusion-based network is proposed that enables the generation of 3D microstructures directly from textual descriptions. Unlike traditional property conditioning methods, our approach leverages rich guidance in terms of semantics and physical properties in the text input to support diverse structure synthesis. To enforce consistency between the generated structures and the target textual prompts, a dual alignment strategy is adopted, including contrastive text-structure alignment and test-time reward-guided alignment. Experimental results show that the model is capable of generating semantically meaningful and physically plausible structures across a wide range of material categories. Our approach has good potential for interactive microstructure design and opens up new directions for combining language-based interfaces with inverse material discovery. Code is available at: https://github.com/hongsong-wang/PropDiff-TMG

AI.tldr: A property-informed diffusion network generates 3D metamaterial microstructures from text descriptions, using dual alignment (contrastive + reward-guided) to ensure semantic and physical consistency.
AI.motivation: Traditional inverse design for microstructures requires domain expertise, iterative simulations, and manual tuning; existing methods suffer from limited diversity and poor physical feasibility.
AI.method: A property-informed diffusion network conditioned on text prompts, with a dual alignment strategy combining contrastive text-structure alignment and test-time reward-guided alignment to enforce consistency.
AI.result: The model generates semantically meaningful and physically plausible 3D microstructures across diverse material categories.
AI.conclusion: This approach enables interactive microstructure design and opens new directions for combining language-based interfaces with inverse material discovery.

title: RAPID: Layer-Wise Redundancy-Aware Pruning and Importance-Driven Token Merging for Efficient ViT
summary: Vision Transformers (ViTs) achieve strong performance but suffer from high computational costs due to quadratic self-attention complexity. Although token reduction techniques such as pruning and merging mitigate this, they typically overlook how representations evolve across network depth. We propose RAPID, a depth-aware token reduction framework that adapts reduction strategies to the layer-wise characteristics of token representations. The primary methodological contribution is a bifurcated strategy: in shallow-to-middle layers, RAPID employs a redundancy-similarity aware pruning metric to eliminate over-represented local patterns. As features transition to global semantic concepts in deeper layers, the framework shifts to an importance-similarity aware merging mechanism. This stage leverages classification (CLS) token attention weights to protect semantically critical tokens while fusing less important but similar neighbors. Empirical validation on ImageNet-1K using ViT and DeiT architectures demonstrates that RAPID establishes a superior accuracy-compression Pareto frontier compared to plug-and-play baselines such as ToMe and ToFu. RAPID is particularly robust in aggressive compression regimes, achieving up to 4.29% higher accuracy than ToMe at extreme reduction rates. Our framework provides a training-free template for optimizing vision models by aligning reduction strategies with hierarchical feature evolution.

AI.tldr: RAPID is a training-free, depth-aware token reduction framework for Vision Transformers that uses pruning in shallow layers and merging in deep layers, achieving superior accuracy-compression trade-offs compared to ToMe and ToFu on ImageNet-1K.
AI.motivation: ViTs suffer from quadratic self-attention complexity. Existing token reduction methods (pruning/merging) ignore how token representations evolve across network depth, leading to suboptimal accuracy-compression trade-offs.
AI.method: RAPID employs a bifurcated depth-aware strategy: (1) shallow-to-middle layers use redundancy-similarity aware pruning to eliminate over-represented local patterns; (2) deeper layers shift to importance-similarity aware merging, leveraging CLS token attention weights to protect critical tokens while fusing less important similar neighbors.
AI.result: On ImageNet-1K with ViT/DeiT, RAPID achieves superior accuracy-compression Pareto frontier vs. ToMe and ToFu; up to 4.29% higher accuracy than ToMe at extreme reduction rates.
AI.conclusion: RAPID provides a training-free template for optimizing vision models by aligning reduction strategies with hierarchical feature evolution.

title: How Much MRI Preprocessing Is Enough? A Cost-Utility Study for Brain MRI Foundation Models
summary: MRI preprocessing defines the input distribution seen by brain MRI foundation models, yet it is usually treated as routine data cleaning rather than a modeling choice. We ask how much preprocessing is worth its computational cost for self-supervised 3D MRI pretraining. Keeping the corpus, 3D ViT backbone, masking protocol, and downstream evaluations fixed, we compare a graded P0-P7 preprocessing spectrum for masked autoencoding (MAE) and joint-embedding predictive learning (JEPA) on 20,000 heterogeneous brain MRI volumes, then transfer the encoders to IDH prediction, MCI classification, brain age regression, and GLI/PED tumor segmentation. The results do not support a simple "more is better" rule. P0/P1 are numerically unstable, making P2 the lowest-cost feasible level; beyond P2, choosing the best feasible preprocessing level improves aggregate utility by only 3.4 percentage points for MAE and 1.8 percentage points for JEPA, with most paired gains statistically unresolved. Stronger preprocessing is beneficial only in selected regimes: IDH improves modestly, AGE and GLI/PED are often near or best at P2, and MCI shows the clearest empirical P7 gain. Cross-level MCI transfer further shows that much of the P7 advantage can be recovered by applying stronger preprocessing downstream, without requiring P7 throughout pretraining. These findings recast MRI preprocessing as a downstream-aware cost-utility decision rather than a default escalation pipeline. Code is available at https://github.com/PangJiangShuan/PreBrain.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the impact of MRI preprocessing levels on self-supervised 3D brain MRI pretraining, finding that moderate preprocessing (P2) is cost-effective and stronger preprocessing yields marginal or uneven gains across downstream tasks.
AI.motivation: MRI preprocessing is typically treated as routine data cleaning rather than a modeling choice, and its impact on self-supervised pretraining for brain MRI foundation models has not been systematically evaluated.
AI.method: The authors compare a graded P0-P7 preprocessing spectrum for MAE and JEPA on 20,000 heterogeneous brain MRI volumes, keeping corpus, 3D ViT backbone, masking protocol, and downstream evaluations fixed, then transfer encoders to four tasks: IDH prediction, MCI classification, brain age regression, and tumor segmentation.
AI.result: No simple "more is better" pattern; P0/P1 are numerically unstable; P2 is the lowest feasible level; beyond P2, aggregate utility gain is only 3.4 pp (MAE) and 1.8 pp (JEPA); stronger preprocessing benefits only specific tasks (MCI shows the clearest gain at P7); P7 advantages can be largely recovered by applying stronger preprocessing downstream.
AI.conclusion: MRI preprocessing should be treated as a downstream-aware cost-utility decision rather than a default escalation pipeline.

title: Empowering Feed-Forward Reconstruction Models with Metric Scale via Satellite Images
summary: Feed-forward 3D reconstruction models have recently shown strong generalization across diverse scenes, yet most of them recover geometry only up to an unknown global scale. This scale ambiguity limits their use in applications that require metric understanding of the environment. Existing metric reconstruction methods commonly rely on large-scale metric annotations or accurate camera calibration, both of which are costly or unreliable in many real-world settings. We propose a satellite-guided framework for resolving scale ambiguity in feed-forward 3D reconstruction. The key idea is to use readily available satellite imagery as a global metric reference. Given a coarse camera pose, our method retrieves a local satellite patch and integrates it with a feed-forward reconstruction backbone through bidirectional cross-view interaction. By enforcing consistency between the reconstructed scene and the satellite reference, the model infers absolute scale, refines scene geometry, and estimates camera pose in a metric coordinate frame. Experiments on KITTI, nuScenes, and Oxford RobotCar show consistent improvements in metric depth estimation, multi-view point-cloud reconstruction, and cross-view camera localization, while preserving strong generalization across datasets and geographic regions.

AI.tldr: A satellite-guided framework that resolves scale ambiguity in feed-forward 3D reconstruction by using satellite imagery as a global metric reference, enabling metric-scale geometry and pose estimation without costly annotations or calibration.
AI.motivation: Feed-forward 3D reconstruction models suffer from unknown global scale ambiguity, and existing metric methods rely on expensive metric annotations or accurate camera calibration, which are impractical in many real-world settings.
AI.method: Retrieves a local satellite patch using a coarse camera pose, integrates it with a feed-forward reconstruction backbone via bidirectional cross-view interaction, and enforces consistency between the reconstructed scene and satellite reference to infer absolute scale, refine geometry, and estimate metric camera pose.
AI.result: Consistent improvements on KITTI, nuScenes, and Oxford RobotCar for metric depth estimation, multi-view point-cloud reconstruction, and cross-view camera localization, while maintaining strong generalization across datasets and regions.
AI.conclusion: Satellite imagery is a practical and scalable global reference for resolving scale ambiguity, enabling metric 3D reconstruction without costly annotations or calibration.

title: SegmentAnyTreeV2: Scaling Transformer-Based Tree Instance Segmentation Across Sensors, Platforms, and Forests
summary: We present SegmentAnyTreeV2, a sensor- and platform-agnostic framework for semantic and instance segmentation of forest point clouds. The model combines a serialization-based Point Transformer v3 backbone with a lightweight semantic head and a tree-focused cross-attention mask decoder. Semantic predictions restrict instance decoding to tree-class voxels, while instance-aware query initialization, one-to-many seed supervision, and asymmetric mask scoring improve separation in dense and structurally complex stands. We further introduce FOR-instance v3, an expanded benchmark comprising 427 scenes and 26,496 annotated trees across diverse biomes, forest structures, and LiDAR platforms. On the FOR-instanceV2 test split, SegmentAnyTreeV2 achieves 90.5% precision, 80.2% recall, 85.0% F1, 90.7% coverage, and 87.6% semantic mIoU, outperforming previous learning-based methods in both instance detection and mask completeness. Zero-shot evaluation on independent sites further demonstrates strong cross-domain generalization.

AI.tldr: SegmentAnyTreeV2 is a sensor-agnostic framework for semantic and instance segmentation of forest point clouds, using a Point Transformer v3 backbone with specialized mask decoding and a new FOR-instance v3 benchmark (427 scenes, 26,496 trees). It achieves 85.0% F1 and 87.6% semantic mIoU, outperforming prior methods with strong cross-domain generalization.
AI.motivation: To enable accurate and generalizable tree segmentation from forest point clouds across diverse sensors, platforms, and forest structures—addressing limitations of prior methods in dense and complex stands.
AI.method: Serialization-based Point Transformer v3 backbone + lightweight semantic head + tree-focused cross-attention mask decoder; instance-aware query initialization, one-to-many seed supervision, asymmetric mask scoring; coupled with a large-scale benchmark (FOR-instance v3: 427 scenes, 26,496 trees).
AI.result: 90.5% precision, 80.2% recall, 85.0% F1, 90.7% coverage, 87.6% semantic mIoU on FOR-instanceV2 test split; outperforms prior learning-based methods; strong zero-shot cross-domain generalization.
AI.conclusion: SegmentAnyTreeV2 provides a robust, platform-agnostic solution for tree instance segmentation with state-of-the-art performance and strong generalization to unseen forest environments.

title: Test-Time Scaling in Multimodal Foundation Models: A Comprehensive Survey of Generation and Reasoning
summary: Test-time Scaling (TTS) has emerged as a pivotal research direction for enhancing model performance by dynamically allocating computational resources during inference. Recent advancements have adapted this paradigm to Multimodal Foundation Models (MFMs), unlocking their potential in multimodal reasoning and generation. Despite rapid progress, the field lacks a systematic survey and unified theoretical framework to delineate the developmental landscape of multimodal TTS. To bridge this gap, we present the first comprehensive review of TTS research for MFMs, proposing a unified taxonomic framework that categorizes existing methodologies into three distinct strategies: sampling-based, feedback-based, and search-based approaches. We further summarize representative applications and benchmarks commonly utilized to evaluate multimodal TTS capabilities in generation and reasoning tasks. Finally, this survey discusses open challenges and outlines future research directions, providing a systematic roadmap for subsequent studies in this rapidly evolving field.

AI.tldr: This paper provides the first comprehensive survey of Test-time Scaling (TTS) for Multimodal Foundation Models (MFMs), proposing a unified taxonomy of three strategies (sampling-based, feedback-based, search-based) and reviewing benchmarks, applications, and future directions.
AI.motivation: Despite rapid progress in adapting Test-time Scaling (TTS) to Multimodal Foundation Models (MFMs), the field lacks a systematic survey and unified theoretical framework to delineate the developmental landscape of multimodal TTS.
AI.method: The authors conduct a comprehensive review and propose a unified taxonomic framework categorizing TTS methods into three strategies: sampling-based, feedback-based, and search-based approaches.
AI.result: A unified taxonomy of multimodal TTS methods, summarization of representative applications and benchmarks for generation and reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: The survey provides a systematic roadmap for subsequent studies and outlines open challenges and future research directions in multimodal TTS.

title: Light-WAM: Efficient World Action Models with State-Fusion Action Decoding
summary: World Action Models (WAMs) extend robot policy learning by incorporating future prediction as an additional training objective, encouraging the policy to encode task-relevant temporal structure in its representations. Current WAMs often rely on large-scale generative architectures that incur high training costs and inference latency, making them difficult to deploy as efficient closed-loop policies. We propose Light-WAM, a lightweight World Action Model for efficient robot manipulation. Specifically, it is built with a compact video backbone and performs future-video supervision in a downsampled latent space, reducing the cost of video co-training while retaining its benefits for representation learning. For action prediction, Light-WAM introduces the StateFusionActionExpert, which reads adapted states from multiple backbone layers, fuses them through learned-query pooling, and directly predicts action chunks in a single forward pass. This design provides an efficient interface between video backbone representations and robot actions, avoiding the need for heavy generative action experts. Experiments demonstrate that Light-WAM maintains strong performance on LIBERO and achieves usable multi-task performance on RoboTwin 2.0, while using only 0.44B trainable parameters. It also achieves 72.03ms inference latency with 4.1GiB peak GPU memory and improved training throughput.

AI.tldr: Light-WAM is a lightweight World Action Model that uses a compact video backbone, latent-space future prediction, and an efficient StateFusionActionExpert to achieve strong robot manipulation performance with only 0.44B parameters, 72ms inference latency, and 4.1GiB GPU memory.
AI.motivation: Current World Action Models rely on large-scale generative architectures with high training costs and inference latency, making them impractical as efficient closed-loop policies. The authors aim to reduce these costs while retaining the benefits of future-prediction for representation learning.
AI.method: Light-WAM uses a compact video backbone with future-video supervision in a downsampled latent space. It introduces StateFusionActionExpert, which reads adapted states from multiple backbone layers, fuses them via learned-query pooling, and predicts action chunks in a single forward pass, avoiding heavy generative action experts.
AI.result: Light-WAM maintains strong performance on LIBERO and achieves usable multi-task performance on RoboTwin 2.0 with only 0.44B trainable parameters. It achieves 72.03ms inference latency, 4.1GiB peak GPU memory, and improved training throughput.
AI.conclusion: Light-WAM demonstrates that efficient, lightweight WAMs can compete with larger models while offering significant advantages in inference speed and memory usage, making them more practical for real-world robot deployment.

title: TIDE: Task-Isolated Diffusion for Unified Video Editing and Generation
summary: Recent advances in Diffusion Transformers have driven rapid progress in video generation and editing, yet these capabilities are still handled by separate, task-specific models. Building a unified framework that supports diverse video tasks remains an open challenge: existing unified attempts either require dedicated auxiliary encoders or lack explicit mechanisms to distinguish heterogeneous conditioning tokens, struggling when the number and type of visual conditions vary across tasks. We propose TIDE, a unified framework that integrates instruction-based editing, reference-guided editing, and multi-reference generation. At its core, we introduce per-token task embeddings that assign each input token a task-specific identifier, enabling the model to explicitly disambiguate target, source, and reference tokens. To simultaneously capture high-level semantic understanding and fine-grained structural fidelity, we design a dual-path conditioning scheme that couples a vision-language model with a VAE latent path for complementary signals. We further devise a multi-task progressive training strategy that incrementally introduces tasks of increasing complexity, effectively harmonizing diverse objectives and enabling smooth generalization across heterogeneous task distributions. Extensive experiments on multiple video editing and generation benchmarks demonstrate that TIDE achieves state-of-the-art performance across all evaluated tasks. Our project page is available at https://LittleWork123.github.io/tide.

AI.tldr: TIDE is a unified diffusion transformer framework that handles instruction-based editing, reference-guided editing, and multi-reference generation by introducing per-token task embeddings, a dual-path conditioning scheme (vision-language model + VAE latent path), and a multi-task progressive training strategy, achieving SOTA on multiple video editing/generation benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current video generation and editing tasks are handled by separate, task-specific models. Building a unified framework is challenging because existing attempts need dedicated auxiliary encoders or lack mechanisms to distinguish heterogeneous conditioning tokens, struggling when visual conditions vary across tasks.
AI.method: (1) Per-token task embeddings assign each input token a task-specific identifier to disambiguate target, source, and reference tokens. (2) Dual-path conditioning scheme couples a vision-language model with a VAE latent path for complementary signals (semantic understanding + structural fidelity). (3) Multi-task progressive training strategy incrementally introduces tasks of increasing complexity to harmonize diverse objectives.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multiple video editing and generation benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art performance across all evaluated tasks (instruction-based editing, reference-guided editing, and multi-reference generation).
AI.conclusion: TIDE successfully unifies multiple video tasks in a single framework, showing that explicit per-token task disambiguation and dual-path conditioning can effectively handle heterogeneous task distributions without requiring dedicated auxiliary encoders.

title: Remember with Confidence: Uncertainty Quantification for Spatio-temporal Memory with Probabilistic Guarantees
summary: Long-horizon robot operation requires spatio-temporal memory to record the environment state and recall it for downstream reasoning. Scene graphs and retrieval-augmented systems ground VLM descriptions to persistent 3D entities with rich semantic descriptions. However, VLM captions are noisy and viewpoint-inconsistent, and existing systems treat them as an oracle with no mechanism to detect unreliable stored descriptions. We introduce object-level semantic uncertainty for multi-view VLM memory: a score that measures object-centric cross-view semantic scatter of captions and identifies semantically unresolved objects. Then, we include our uncertainty scores in an advanced spatial-semantic memory system, that we dub UQ-DAAAM. UQ-DAAAM uses this score to actively refine uncertain objects under a fixed query budget by selecting high-quality views and fusing the resulting multi-view captions into a single object description. We also derive probabilistic guarantees showing that higher-quality candidate views (as selected by our approach) are more likely to reduce uncertainty. Our experiments show that uncertainty quantification can make embodied 4D memory systems more reliable and more effective. In particular, on the OC-NaVQA benchmark, UQ-DAAAM achieves substantially larger uncertainty reduction and better spatio-temporal question answering performance than baselines.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces UQ-DAAAM, a spatial-semantic memory system that uses object-level uncertainty scores to detect unreliable VLM captions and actively refine them by selecting high-quality views, improving long-horizon robot operation and question answering.
AI.motivation: Existing VLM-based memory systems treat captions as reliable oracles with no mechanism to detect noisy, viewpoint-inconsistent descriptions, making them unreliable for long-horizon robot operation.
AI.method: The authors propose object-level semantic uncertainty scores to measure cross-view semantic scatter, then integrate these scores into UQ-DAAAM, which actively refines uncertain objects under a fixed query budget by selecting high-quality views and fusing multi-view captions.
AI.result: UQ-DAAAM achieves substantially larger uncertainty reduction and better spatio-temporal question answering performance than baselines on the OC-NaVQA benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty quantification can make embodied 4D memory systems more reliable and effective for long-horizon robot tasks.

title: G2G: Exploiting Intra-Group Geometry for Inter-Group Pose Estimation
summary: Recovering the relative 6-DoF pose between two image groups underlies cross-sequence relocalization and multi-camera rig odometry. Each group carries known intra-group geometry from visual odometry or rig calibration, and pretrained multi-view backbones already fuse such geometry into visual features. Yet current models treat all views as an unstructured set, leaving cross-group reasoning as the missing piece. We introduce \ours{}, which keeps the foundation model entirely frozen and adds three lightweight trainable modules to bridge the two groups: a perceiver resampler, a cross-group bridge with merged self-attention, and a multi-frame pose head. The trainable footprint totals about 32M parameters, under 6\% of the full model, and is supervised only by relative poses. Across four datasets that span indoor and outdoor simulation, real-world cross-season capture, and zero-shot sim-to-real transfer, \ours{} attains state-of-the-art accuracy on both tasks, while every baseline is retrained with its full original supervision. Code is available at https://github.com/WeiYuFei0217/G2G.

AI.tldr: G2G introduces a method to recover 6-DoF pose between two image groups by freezing a multi-view foundation model and adding three lightweight trainable modules (perceiver resampler, cross-group bridge with merged self-attention, multi-frame pose head), totaling ~32M parameters. It achieves SOTA on cross-sequence relocalization and multi-camera rig odometry across four datasets.
AI.motivation: Current multi-view models treat all views as an unstructured set and lack explicit cross-group reasoning, which is needed for tasks like cross-sequence relocalization and multi-camera rig odometry where intra-group geometry is known but inter-group pose must be inferred.
AI.method: Freezes a pretrained multi-view foundation model entirely, adds three lightweight trainable modules: (1) a perceiver resampler to compress per-group features, (2) a cross-group bridge with merged self-attention to enable inter-group communication, and (3) a multi-frame pose head to regress relative 6-DoF pose. Trained with only relative pose supervision (~32M trainable params, <6% of full model).
AI.result: Attains state-of-the-art accuracy on both cross-sequence relocalization and multi-camera rig odometry across four datasets (indoor/outdoor simulation, real-world cross-season, zero-shot sim-to-real), outperforming baselines retrained with full supervision.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight trainable modules added to a frozen multi-view foundation model can effectively bridge two image groups for relative pose estimation, achieving superior accuracy with minimal trainable parameters and without requiring full model retraining.

title: HACK++: Towards More Effective Head-Aware Key-Value Compression for Efficient Visual Autoregressive Modeling
summary: Visual Autoregressive (VAR) models adopt a next-scale prediction paradigm, offering high-quality generation with substantially fewer decoding steps. However, existing VAR models suffer from significant attention complexity and severe memory overhead due to the accumulation of key-value (KV) caches across scales. In this paper, we tackle this challenge by introducing KV cache compression into the next-scale paradigm. We begin with an in-depth analysis of VAR attention and observe that attention heads can be stably divided into two functionally distinct categories: Contextual Heads focus on maintaining semantic consistency, while Structural Heads preserve spatial coherence. Their functional divergence makes existing one-size-fits-all compression methods perform poorly on VAR models. We further find that the two head types differ markedly in their reliance on historical scales, and that this reliance shifts across layers and generation steps, arguing for an adaptive cache budget allocation. To address these challenges, we propose HACK++, a training-free Head-Aware key-value Compression frameworK for VAR models. From a one-time offline calibration, HACK++ classifies head types and derives head-specific priors. At inference, it decouples attention from cache compression under independent budgets, bounding the current-scale attention cost while compressing the accumulated cache far more aggressively, via pattern-specific strategies and a reliance-aware budget allocation. Extensive experiments on multiple VAR models across text-to-image, class-conditional, and unified understanding-and-generation tasks validate the effectiveness and generalizability of HACK++. For example, on Infinity-2B/8B, HACK++ maintains near-lossless generation with only a 30% attention budget and a 10% cache budget, and remains robust even under a 1% cache budget.

AI.tldr: HACK++ is a training-free KV cache compression framework for VAR models that classifies attention heads into Contextual (semantic) and Structural (spatial) types, then applies adaptive, head-specific compression strategies to drastically reduce memory and computation overhead while maintaining near-lossless generation quality.
AI.motivation: Existing VAR models suffer from high attention complexity and severe memory overhead due to KV cache accumulation across scales, and existing one-size-fits-all compression methods perform poorly because VAR attention heads divide into two functionally distinct types (Contextual vs. Structural) with different compression needs.
AI.method: HACK++ uses a one-time offline calibration to classify head types and derive head-specific priors. At inference, it decouples attention from cache compression under independent budgets, employing pattern-specific strategies and reliance-aware budget allocation to compress the accumulated cache aggressively while bounding current-scale attention cost.
AI.result: On Infinity-2B/8B, HACK++ achieves near-lossless generation with only 30% attention budget and 10% cache budget, and remains robust even under extreme 1% cache budget. Validated across text-to-image, class-conditional, and unified understanding-and-generation tasks.
AI.conclusion: HACK++ effectively addresses the KV cache bottleneck in VAR models through head-aware, adaptive compression, demonstrating strong generalizability and robustness across diverse tasks and model scales.

title: Set-Based Transformer for Atmospheric Compensation in Standoff LWIR Hyperspectral Imaging
summary: Passive long-wave infrared (LWIR) hyperspectral imaging under a standoff geometry depends on atmospheric absorption and emission, as well as reflected radiance, thus making atmospheric compensation essential to get knowledge of a target of interest. Despite its importance, this compensation has been largely overlooked due to its practical and modeling difficulty. In this paper, we present a lightweight set-based deep learning framework that takes multiple radiance measurements, collected at different standoff ranges, as input and jointly estimates transmittance, atmospheric path radiance, and a shared downwelling spectrum. We analyze the learned representation with a sparse autoencoder and observe that several latent features do activate on geographically coherent subsets of the test data despite the absence of location supervision. Experiments on a MODTRAN generated standoff LWIR dataset demonstrate low spectral distortion across all estimated products. The dataset and code is publicly available at: https://factral.co/SAE-LWIR/

AI.tldr: The paper presents a lightweight deep learning framework for atmospheric compensation in passive long-wave infrared hyperspectral imaging, using multiple radiance measurements at different standoff ranges to jointly estimate transmittance, path radiance, and downwelling spectrum, with experiments showing low spectral distortion.
AI.motivation: Atmospheric compensation in passive LWIR hyperspectral imaging is essential but has been largely overlooked due to practical and modeling difficulties, despite being critical for understanding targets of interest under standoff geometry.
AI.method: A lightweight set-based deep learning framework that takes multiple radiance measurements at different standoff ranges as input, jointly estimating transmittance, atmospheric path radiance, and a shared downwelling spectrum. The learned representation is analyzed using a sparse autoencoder.
AI.result: Low spectral distortion across all estimated products on a MODTRAN-generated standoff LWIR dataset. Latent features activate on geographically coherent subsets of test data despite no location supervision.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method effectively addresses atmospheric compensation in LWIR hyperspectral imaging, with promising results and publicly available dataset/code for reproducibility.

title: SMI: Efficient Self-Supervised Learning via Mutual-Information-Inspired Dependency Optimization
summary: Self-supervised learning (SSL) has achieved remarkable representation learning performance, but many existing methods rely on large batch sizes, memory banks, momentum encoders, or global synchronization mechanisms that substantially increase computational cost and training complexity. In this work, we propose Semantic Mutual Information (SMI), a lightweight self-supervised objective derived from a mutual-information-inspired dependency formulation under Gaussian assumptions. Unlike conventional correlation matching objectives that operate on high-dimensional feature correlation matrices, SMI performs optimization on a sample-level dependency matrix through a nonlinear transformation of pairwise correlations. This formulation induces distinct optimization dynamics that emphasize strongly dependent semantic pairs while maintaining representation diversity. Experimental results on ImageNet using a ResNet-50 backbone demonstrate that SMI achieves competitive linear evaluation performance relative to state-of-the-art SSL approaches while substantially reducing computational complexity. Across multiple low-resource benchmarks, SMI consistently improves transfer performance over Barlow Twins, particularly on fine-grained datasets. Furthermore, analyses of optimization dynamics and representation geometry suggest improved alignment--redundancy balance, greater feature diversity, and more spatially localized semantic representations. These results indicate that nonlinear dependency optimization provides an effective and computationally efficient alternative to conventional correlation-based self-supervised learning objectives.

AI.tldr: SMI proposes a lightweight self-supervised learning objective that uses nonlinear dependency optimization on sample-level correlation matrices, achieving competitive performance to state-of-the-art SSL methods with lower computational cost.
AI.motivation: Existing SSL methods require large batch sizes, memory banks, momentum encoders, or global synchronization, which increase computational cost and training complexity.
AI.method: Semantic Mutual Information (SMI) — a mutual-information-inspired objective that performs optimization on a sample-level dependency matrix via nonlinear transformation of pairwise correlations under Gaussian assumptions.
AI.result: SMI achieves competitive linear evaluation on ImageNet (ResNet-50) with lower complexity, improves transfer on low-resource and fine-grained datasets over Barlow Twins, and shows better alignment-redundancy balance, feature diversity, and spatially localized representations.
AI.conclusion: Nonlinear dependency optimization is an effective and computationally efficient alternative to conventional correlation-based SSL objectives.

title: Beyond Raw Signals: Undecoded Generative Latents as Privileged Synthetic Data
summary: While multimodal integration significantly improves computer vision models, deploying them incurs prohibitive inference costs and requires scarce, perfectly paired datasets. Recent methods address this data bottleneck by synthesizing missing modalities via generative AI, yet they introduce a severe inefficiency: the Decode-Encode Loop. Specifically, information-rich generative latents are decoded into noisy raw signals, forcing the downstream classifier to waste capacity re-encoding them. To bypass this bottleneck, we propose Direct Latent Augmentation (DLA), utilizing undecoded generative latents directly as privileged information. Furthermore, to transfer this dense knowledge to a purely visual student, we introduce Multilayer Explicit Simulated Synesthesia (MESSy). Instead of enforcing rigid representation matching, which forces the student to distort its native visual features to accommodate complex multimodal topologies, MESSy uses a predictive objective to safely internalize these physical priors. Empirical results demonstrate that our framework significantly outperforms raw data augmentation and traditional distillation. Ultimately, our approach yields highly accurate unimodal students with ``synesthetic'' latent structures that are inherently aligned with physical properties they have never directly observed.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Direct Latent Augmentation (DLA) to avoid the inefficient decode-encode loop in multimodal learning, and Multilayer Explicit Simulated Synesthesia (MESSy) to transfer privileged generative latent knowledge to visual-only students via predictive objectives, achieving strong performance without paired data.
AI.motivation: Multimodal integration improves vision models but suffers from high inference costs and scarce paired datasets. Existing generative methods waste computation by decoding generative latents to noise and re-encoding them (Decode-Encode Loop).
AI.method: DLA uses undecoded generative latents as privileged information. MESSy transfers this knowledge to a visual student via predictive objectives (rather than rigid representation matching), enabling "simulated synesthesia."
AI.result: Outperforms raw data augmentation and traditional distillation. Produces accurate unimodal students with latent structures aligned to unobserved physical properties.
AI.conclusion: Bypassing the decode-encode loop and using predictive distillation yields more efficient and effective multimodal-to-unimodal knowledge transfer.

title: Self-Supervised Vision Transformers for CBCT-Based Detection of Temporomandibular Joint Osteoarthritis
summary: Temporomandibular joint osteoarthritis (TMJ OA) is a prevalent degenerative condition whose osseous changes are often subtle on cone-beam CT (CBCT), making automated detection challenging. We study how well the DINO family of self-supervised vision transformers -- DINOv1, DINOv2, DINOv2+reg, and RAD-DINO (a radiology-pretrained variant) -- transfers to CBCT, asking how much backbone adaptation is needed and of what kind. We propose a simple slice-based pipeline using Vision Transformer (ViT) backbones: axial CBCT slices are encoded per-slice by a frozen or partially adapted ViT and aggregated via attention-based multiple instance learning (MIL) for patient-level binary OA/Normal classification. Through systematic ablation across unfreezing strategies and aggregation designs on a multi-source CBCT dataset, we find that partial unfreezing of the final two transformer blocks is the decisive factor, improving AUC from 0.671 (fully frozen DINOv2) to 0.902. This outperforms DINOv1 (0.867), DINOv2+reg (0.774), and a supervised ImageNet ViT-B/16 baseline (0.843). Our results provide practical guidance for adapting DINO-family foundation models in low-data medical imaging settings, showing that adaptation strategy is a stronger driver of performance than backbone choice alone.

AI.tldr: This paper studies DINO-family self-supervised vision transformers for TMJ osteoarthritis detection on CBCT images, finding that partially unfreezing the final two transformer blocks dramatically improves AUC from 0.671 to 0.902, outperforming other backbones and a supervised baseline.
AI.motivation: TMJ OA osseous changes are often subtle on CBCT, making automated detection challenging. The authors want to understand how well DINO-family models transfer to this low-data medical imaging domain and what adaptation strategies are needed.
AI.method: A slice-based pipeline using ViT backbones (DINOv1, DINOv2, DINOv2+reg, RAD-DINO) with frozen or partially adapted encoders, aggregating per-slice features via attention-based multiple instance learning (MIL) for patient-level binary classification. Systematic ablation of unfreezing strategies and aggregation designs.
AI.result: Partial unfreezing of final two transformer blocks was decisive, boosting AUC from 0.671 to 0.902, outperforming DINOv1 (0.867), DINOv2+reg (0.774), and supervised ImageNet ViT-B/16 (0.843).
AI.conclusion: Adaptation strategy matters more than backbone choice for DINO-family models in low-data medical imaging; partial fine-tuning of final blocks is highly effective.

title: SceneConductor: 3D Scene Generation from Single Image with Multi-Agent Orchestration
summary: Generating complete 3D scenes from a single image requires inferring globally consistent geometry, object relationships, and environmental context from inherently ambiguous visual evidence. Despite recent progress in joint layout-and-mesh generation, existing methods often rely on holistic or weakly decomposed pipelines that entangle many factors at once and demand extensive scene-level supervision, limiting their generalization to complex real-world environments. We propose a multi-agent orchestration framework that decomposes single-image 3D scene generation into three structured stages: scene initialization, environment construction, and multi-agent refinement. The initialization stage extracts image-derived object masks, builds object-level 3D representations, and predicts an initial spatial layout to form a coarse 3D scene. The environment-construction stage then leverages this initialization together with point-map geometry to build an environmental scaffold of supporting surfaces, room boundaries, materials, and illumination. Finally, in the refinement stage, a planner agent identifies structural and visual inconsistencies, applies simple corrections directly, and dispatches specialist agents for complex localized revisions that are reintegrated into the global scene. To provide reliable structural initialization while reducing reliance on scene-level annotations, we further introduce a geometry-aware layout predictor supervised by sparse geometric priors derived from point maps. Unlike fully supervised layout generators, the predictor can be trained from segmentation-level data and generalizes robustly to diverse real-world scenes. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets show that our method consistently outperforms prior approaches in geometric accuracy, spatial consistency, and perceptual realism.

AI.tldr: A multi-agent framework decomposes single-image 3D scene generation into three stages—scene initialization, environment construction, and multi-agent refinement—achieving superior geometric accuracy and realism while reducing reliance on scene-level supervision.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for single-image 3D scene generation rely on holistic pipelines that entangle multiple factors and require extensive scene-level supervision, limiting generalization to complex real-world environments.
AI.method: A multi-agent orchestration framework with three stages: (1) initialization (object masks, 3D representations, layout prediction), (2) environment construction (surfaces, boundaries, materials, illumination using point-map geometry), (3) multi-agent refinement (planner agent fixes simple issues, specialist agents handle complex localized revisions). Also introduces a geometry-aware layout predictor trained with sparse geometric priors from point maps.
AI.result: Outperforms prior methods on benchmark datasets in geometric accuracy, spatial consistency, and perceptual realism.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-agent decomposition and geometry-aware layout prediction effectively address generalization challenges, reducing the need for full scene-level supervision while improving 3D scene quality.

title: Geometry-Driven Flow Analysis of Brain Sulcal Pattern
summary: Cortical folding reflects coordinated neurodevelopmental processes and is increasingly recognized as a sensitive marker of neurological disease. However, most existing analyses rely on indirect scalar summaries that do not explicitly model folding geometry itself. In juvenile myoclonic epilepsy (JME), a common genetic epilepsy, cortical abnormalities are often subtle, spatially distributed, and difficult to detect using conventional morphometric measures. We introduce a Poisson-equation-based framework that models cortical folding as a geometry-driven flow derived from mean curvature on the cortical manifold. By treating folding patterns as a stationary source-sink structure, the proposed approach yields a smooth, globally balanced potential field whose surface gradient defines a physically interpretable flux. This framework enables spatially coherent analysis of sulcal-gyral folding organization and provides a principled representation of geometry-driven cortical structure in JME.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a Poisson-equation-based framework that models cortical folding as a geometry-driven flow to detect subtle, spatially distributed cortical abnormalities in juvenile myoclonic epilepsy (JME).
AI.motivation: Cortical folding abnormalities in JME are often subtle and spatially distributed, making them difficult to detect with conventional morphometric measures that rely on indirect scalar summaries rather than explicitly modeling folding geometry.
AI.method: A Poisson-equation-based framework that models cortical folding as a geometry-driven flow derived from mean curvature on the cortical manifold, treating folding patterns as a stationary source-sink structure to produce a smooth potential field with an interpretable surface gradient flux.
AI.result: The framework yields a smooth, globally balanced potential field whose surface gradient defines a physically interpretable flux, enabling spatially coherent analysis of sulcal-gyral folding organization.
AI.conclusion: This approach provides a principled, geometry-driven representation of cortical structure in JME, potentially improving detection of subtle folding abnormalities beyond conventional morphometric measures.

title: CoVEBench: Can Video Editing Models Handle Complex Instructions?
summary: While recent text-guided video editing models excel at elementary tasks (e.g., style transfer, object insertion), real-world user requests are highly compositional. A single prompt often demands multiple coupled edits, such as modifying subjects, actions, and camera views, while strictly preserving unrelated spatiotemporal content. Existing benchmarks, heavily constrained by isolated edits and coarse global metrics, fail to diagnose how models handle such complex workflows. To address this gap, we introduce CoVEBench, a compositional video editing benchmark comprising 416 curated source videos, 626 multi-point editing instructions, and 9,990 fine-grained checklist items. Covering diverse editing dimensions, CoVEBench evaluates models via MLLM-judged instruction compliance and video fidelity, alongside automated metrics for video quality. Extensive experiments reveal that compositional editing remains a profound challenge: current models frequently omit edits, violate preservation constraints, or introduce artifacts when handling multiple operations simultaneously. CoVEBench provides a challenging, diagnostic testbed to advance video editing toward realistic user workflows.

AI.tldr: CoVEBench is a new benchmark for compositional video editing, containing 416 videos, 626 multi-point instructions, and 9,990 checklist items. It reveals current models struggle with multiple simultaneous edits, often omitting changes, violating preservation constraints, or introducing artifacts.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks are limited to isolated edits and coarse global metrics, failing to diagnose how models handle real-world compositional user requests that involve multiple coupled edits (e.g., modifying subjects, actions, and camera views simultaneously) while preserving unrelated content.
AI.method: The authors construct CoVEBench with 416 curated source videos, 626 multi-point editing instructions, and 9,990 fine-grained checklist items. They use MLLM-judged instruction compliance and video fidelity, along with automated quality metrics, to evaluate models across diverse editing dimensions.
AI.result: Compositional editing remains a profound challenge for current models, which frequently omit edits, violate preservation constraints, or introduce artifacts when handling multiple simultaneous operations.
AI.conclusion: CoVEBench provides a challenging, diagnostic testbed to advance video editing toward realistic user workflows by revealing critical shortcomings in existing models.

title: CheXanatomy: Anatomy-Aware Vision-Language Modeling for Chest Radiographs
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) pretrained on large-scale image-text pairs demonstrate strong image-level understanding, but are primarily optimized for global alignment and do not explicitly encode fine-grained anatomical structure, limiting their suitability for spatially precise tasks such as segmentation. We introduce CheXanatomy, a framework that integrates explicit anatomical knowledge into a pretrained VLM through autoregressive token-space supervision. Instead of adding task-specific decoder heads, the model is trained to generate anatomical segmentation masks via next-token prediction. To enable scalable supervision, we synthesize realistic chest radiographs from CT volumes and forward-project CT segmentation labels to obtain anatomically consistent 2D masks. We evaluate the approach on synthetic and real chest radiographs against a U-Net baseline, including ablations on model scale, input resolution, and vision encoder fine-tuning. Autoregressive anatomical supervision achieves performance comparable to specialized convolutional models in-distribution and demonstrates improved geometric robustness under domain shift to real CXR data. In addition, anatomy-pretrained models exhibit improved sample efficiency when adapting to novel localization tasks under limited supervision. Larger models and higher input image resolution improve performance, while vision encoder fine-tuning has limited effect. These results show that embedding anatomical structure directly into the generative objective promotes spatially grounded representations and supports anatomy-aware medical vision-language modeling.

AI.tldr: CheXanatomy integrates anatomical knowledge into VLMs via autoregressive token-space supervision for segmentation, showing competitive performance with U-Net and improved robustness under domain shift.
AI.motivation: Existing VLMs excel at global image-level understanding but lack fine-grained anatomical structure encoding, limiting their effectiveness for spatially precise tasks like segmentation.
AI.method: The framework trains a VLM to generate anatomical segmentation masks via next-token prediction using synthetic chest radiographs generated from CT volumes with forward-projected labels.
AI.result: Achieves comparable performance to U-Net in-distribution, improved geometric robustness under domain shift to real CXR data, and better sample efficiency for novel localization tasks.
AI.conclusion: Embedding anatomical structure into the generative objective promotes spatially grounded representations and supports anatomy-aware medical vision-language modeling.

title: Segmentation-Assisted Brain MRI Synthesis with Cross-Image Multi-Contrast Feature Memory Bank Retrieval Augmentation
summary: Multi-contrast brain MRI provide complementary soft-tissue characteristics that aid in the screening and diagnosis of diseases.
