However, limited scanning time, image corruption and various imaging protocols often result in incomplete multi-contrast images. While current approaches excel in image synthesis, they often struggle to synthesize critical tumor regions and exploit contextual information in multi-contrast brain MRI effectively. To address this issue, we propose a synthesis-centric, segmentation-assisted closed-loop framework with retrieval augmentation synthesis. Our method overall takes a generative adversarial architecture, which aims to synthesize missing contrasts from any combination of available ones with a single model. To explicitly capture tumor semantics and focus synthesis on tumor regions, we add an auxiliary segmentation branch that predicts tumor masks and feeds them back as semantic conditioning in synthesis branch, thereby learning tumor-aware representations in the model and improving synthesis fidelity. Furthermore, we propose a dual-bank retrieval augmentation strategy. It dynamically queries two external knowledge bases, namely a tumor masks memory bank for crucial tumor context and cross-image contrast feature memory bank for global style information, to augment synthesis. Verified on two public multi-contrast magnetic resonance brain datasets: BraTs2020 and UCSF-BMSR, the proposed method is effective in handling medical brain images synthesis tasks and shows superior performance compared to previous methods. Code is available at:https://github.com/iBizzard/SSCF.git

AI.tldr: A synthesis-centric, segmentation-assisted closed-loop framework with retrieval augmentation (SSCF) that synthesizes missing brain MRI contrasts using a GAN architecture, segmentation feedback, and dual-bank retrieval for tumor-aware synthesis.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-contrast brain MRI synthesis methods struggle to synthesize critical tumor regions and effectively exploit contextual information, especially when dealing with incomplete contrast images.
AI.method: (1) A single GAN model that synthesizes missing contrasts from any available combination; (2) An auxiliary segmentation branch that predicts tumor masks and feeds them back as semantic conditioning; (3) A dual-bank retrieval augmentation strategy querying tumor masks memory bank and cross-image contrast feature memory bank.
AI.result: Outperforms previous methods on BraTs2020 and UCSF-BMSR datasets in medical brain image synthesis tasks.
AI.conclusion: The closed-loop segmentation-assisted design with retrieval augmentation significantly improves tumor-aware synthesis fidelity for multi-contrast brain MRI.

title: Reinforcing Temporal Answer Grounding in Instructional Video via Candidate-Aware Causal Reasoning
summary: The task of temporal answer grounding in instructional video (TAGV), which aims to locate precise video segments that respond to natural language queries, is increasingly important for direct video answer retrieval. This task remains challenging due to the need to comprehend semantically complex questions and to address the significant length mismatch between untrimmed videos and short target moments. Existing methods often suffer from sensitivity to irrelevant content or insufficient visual reasoning capabilities. To tackle these limitations, we propose a Candidate-Aware Causal Reasoning (CACR) framework. Our approach first employs a Visual-Language Pre-training based Candidate Selection (VBCS) algorithm to efficiently generate K candidate segments, then applies a temporal logic reasoning module enhanced by a rejection reward mechanism and optimized via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) for robust inference. Extensive experiments on six benchmarks demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in terms of mean Intersection-over-Union (mIoU), providing a new perspective for reasoning-based retrieval in long videos.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes CACR, a Candidate-Aware Causal Reasoning framework for temporal answer grounding in instructional videos, which uses a VBCS algorithm to select candidate segments and a temporal logic reasoning module with rejection reward and GRPO optimization, achieving SOTA performance on six benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The task of temporal answer grounding in instructional videos is challenging due to semantically complex questions and significant length mismatch between untrimmed videos and short target moments. Existing methods suffer from sensitivity to irrelevant content and insufficient visual reasoning capabilities.
AI.method: The CACR framework has two main components: (1) a Visual-Language Pre-training based Candidate Selection (VBCS) algorithm to efficiently generate K candidate segments, and (2) a temporal logic reasoning module enhanced by a rejection reward mechanism and optimized via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) for robust inference.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on six benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art performance in terms of mean Intersection-over-Union (mIoU).
AI.conclusion: The proposed CACR framework provides a new perspective for reasoning-based retrieval in long videos, effectively addressing limitations of existing methods.

title: TVI-CoT: Text-Visual Interleaved Chain-of-Thought Reasoning for Multimodal Understanding
summary: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning has proven effective for enhancing problem-solving in large language models. However, when applied to multimodal LLMs (MLLMs), existing CoT approaches suffer from a fundamental limitation: they perform reasoning entirely in text without accessing visual features during the reasoning process. After initial visual encoding, image information becomes inaccessible, forcing models to reason based solely on whatever was captured in the initial description, which forms a `vision-blind reasoning' paradigm that limits fine-grained visual extraction, error verification, and adaptive attention. We propose Text-Visual Interleaved Chain-of-Thought (TVI-CoT), a framework that enables explicit interleaving of textual reasoning and visual feature access through learnable control tokens <THINK>, <LOOK> and <ANSWER>. These tokens allow dynamic switching between reasoning and visual grounding, attending to relevant image regions conditioned on the evolving reasoning state. Experiments on eight benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art results among MLLM-based CoT methods and notable performance boost compared to the baseline: +6.1% on MMMU, +3.8% on MathVerse, +3.4% on MathVista, and +3.4% on ScienceQA. Code is available at https://github.com/hulianyuyy/TVI-CoT.

AI.tldr: TVI-CoT introduces learnable control tokens (<THINK>, <LOOK>, <ANSWER>) enabling multimodal LLMs to interleave textual reasoning with active visual feature access, overcoming the "vision-blind" limitation of standard chain-of-thought reasoning.
AI.motivation: Existing CoT methods for multimodal LLMs perform reasoning entirely in text without accessing visual features after initial encoding, creating a "vision-blind reasoning" paradigm that limits fine-grained visual extraction, error verification, and adaptive attention.
AI.method: Proposes Text-Visual Interleaved Chain-of-Thought (TVI-CoT) with three learnable control tokens: <THINK> for textual reasoning, <LOOK> for dynamic visual feature access conditioned on reasoning state, and <ANSWER> for final output. These tokens enable switching between reasoning and visual grounding.
AI.result: State-of-the-art results among MLLM-based CoT methods on 8 benchmarks; performance boosts over baseline: +6.1% on MMMU, +3.8% on MathVerse, +3.4% on MathVista, +3.4% on ScienceQA.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly interleaving visual access with textual reasoning via control tokens significantly improves multimodal reasoning performance, addressing a fundamental limitation of current CoT approaches.

title: Seeing is Believing: Aligning Prompt Rewriting with Visual Anchors for Text-to-Image Generation
summary: Despite the impressive capabilities of text-to-image (T2I) models, an intent-generation gap often persists due to the brevity and ambiguity of user prompts. Existing approaches primarily polish the prompt for fluency and readability. However, the enhancement process still lacks visual grounding. As a result, the rewriter may over-infer missing details, causing an intent-generation gap. To address this limitation, we propose FaithRewriter, a novel prompt-enhancement framework for T2I generation. Specifically, FaithRewriter first leverages a multimodal MLLM to generate an image from the original prompt as an intermediate visual cue. This cue is then combined with the prompt and fed into a large-scale LLM to produce visually grounded augmentations that better reflect how the intended content should appear in images. Finally, these augmentations are distilled into a small-scale LLM for efficient deployment, enhancing its ability to generate effective T2I prompts. Experiments show that FaithRewriter yields prompts that are more faithful to the user intent and more visually plausible than strong baselines, helping narrow the intent-generation gap.

AI.tldr: FaithRewriter is a prompt-enhancement framework that uses MLLM to generate visual cues, LLM for visually grounded augmentations, and distills into a small LLM for efficient T2I prompt rewriting to narrow the intent-generation gap.
AI.motivation: Existing prompt rewriting methods lack visual grounding and may over-infer missing details, causing an intent-generation gap between user prompts and T2I model outputs.
AI.method: (1) MLLM generates an image from the original prompt as an intermediate visual cue. (2) Visual cue + prompt are fed into a large-scale LLM to produce visually grounded augmentations. (3) Augmentations are distilled into a small-scale LLM for efficient deployment.
AI.result: FaithRewriter produces prompts more faithful to user intent and more visually plausible than strong baselines.
AI.conclusion: Visually grounded prompt enhancement helps narrow the intent-generation gap in text-to-image generation.

title: Look Less, Reason More: Block-wise Attention Skipping for Efficient Multimodal LLMs
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) face a significant inference bottleneck due to the quadratic computational cost of self-attention over long visual token sequences. However, we identify a critical inefficiency in current architectures: Visual Attention Saturation. Our analysis reveals that visual tokens rapidly establish their spatial structure and intra-modal relationships in early layers, rendering visual-to-visual self-attention in deeper layers computationally redundant. Conversely, Feed-Forward Networks (FFNs) in these layers remain essential for projecting visual features into the evolving textual semantic space. Leveraging this insight, we present Visual-Skip (V-Skip), a training-free inference paradigm that decouples spatial interaction from semantic evolution. Rather than discarding tokens, V-Skip imposes block-wise structured sparsity by selectively bypassing saturated visual self-attention modules. Furthermore, recognizing that varying downstream tasks demand distinct reasoning depths, V-Skip employs a lightweight, few-shot calibration to dynamically route the task-optimal sparsity path. Extensive experiments demonstrate that V-Skip effectively bypasses redundant vision attention to achieve block-wise sparsity, maintaining a 94.16% to 100.31% performance retention across diverse MLLMs. Ultimately, we prove that to reason more effectively, models do not need to discard what they see -- they simply need to "look less" at the right depth.

AI.tldr: V-Skip is a training-free inference method that selectively bypasses saturated visual self-attention modules in deeper layers of MLLMs, reducing computational cost while maintaining 94-100% performance across diverse models.
AI.motivation: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) suffer from quadratic inference costs due to self-attention over long visual token sequences. The authors identify that visual-to-visual self-attention becomes computationally redundant in deeper layers after visual tokens establish spatial structure early on.
AI.method: V-Skip introduces block-wise structured sparsity by bypassing saturated visual self-attention modules in deeper layers while keeping FFNs active. It uses lightweight few-shot calibration to dynamically select the optimal sparsity path for different downstream tasks.
AI.result: V-Skip achieves 94.16% to 100.31% performance retention across various MLLMs while reducing redundant computation by skipping saturated visual attention blocks.
AI.conclusion: Models do not need to discard visual tokens to reason more efficiently; they simply need to "look less" at the right depth by bypassing attention that has already saturated.

title: OmniTryOn: Video Try-On Anything at Once!
summary: Although video virtual try-on (VVT) has achieved significant progress, existing methods still exhibit two fundamental limitations: first, they are restricted to single-garment transfer, rendering simultaneous multi-object try-on highly impractical; second, their heavy reliance on explicit external priors (e.g., garment masks) inevitably destroys crucial physical dynamics and degrades visual quality. To bridge this gap, this paper proposes the novel Try-On Anything task, which aims to simultaneously transfer diverse wearable objects onto a person in a video in a single inference pass. To support and standardize this paradigm, we introduce TryAny-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark encompassing a paired video dataset alongside a tailored evaluation protocol. Furthermore, we present OmniTryOn, an external-prior-free generative framework designed to tackle this task. Specifically, OmniTryOn employs a First Frame Wearable Cache strategy, which directly provides diverse wearable objects for the generation process through the initial video frame. To maintain consistency, we propose the Spatiotemporally Consistent RoPE (STC-RoPE), which inherently establishes robust spatiotemporal anchors to strictly preserve complex human motions and background dynamics. Optimized by the proposed Gradual Try-On (GTO) training strategy, our model progressively masters robust multi-object synthesis. Extensive experiments on TryAny-Bench demonstrate that OmniTryOn significantly outperforms existing specialized video virtual try-on models and general video editing baselines, establishing a powerful new standard for the Try-On Anything task. Our dataset, code, and models are available at https://github.com/xcltql666/OminTryOn.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes the Try-On Anything task for simultaneous multi-object video virtual try-on without external priors, introduces TryAny-Bench as a benchmark, and presents OmniTryOn, a generative framework using First Frame Wearable Cache, STC-RoPE, and Gradual Try-On training, outperforming existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing VVT methods are limited to single-garment transfer and rely heavily on external priors (e.g., garment masks), which destroy physical dynamics and degrade visual quality. The authors aim to overcome these limitations by enabling simultaneous multi-object try-on in videos without external priors.
AI.method: OmniTryOn: (1) First Frame Wearable Cache — provides diverse wearable objects from the initial video frame; (2) Spatiotemporally Consistent RoPE (STC-RoPE) — establishes robust spatiotemporal anchors to preserve motion and background dynamics; (3) Gradual Try-On (GTO) training strategy — progressively trains the model for robust multi-object synthesis. Introduces TryAny-Bench benchmark with paired video dataset and evaluation protocol.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on TryAny-Bench show OmniTryOn significantly outperforms existing specialized VVT models and general video editing baselines.
AI.conclusion: OmniTryOn establishes a powerful new standard for the Try-On Anything task. Dataset, code, and models are publicly available.

title: DriveReward: A Comprehensive Dataset and Generative Vision-Language Reward Model for Autonomous Driving
summary: Reward models play a pivotal role in reinforcement learning (RL) and multi-modal trajectory selection for autonomous driving. However, acquiring such rewards typically relies on hand-crafted rule-based objectives or perception ground truth, which hinders generalization for data-scaling. While Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have demonstrated feasibility as reward models in other domains, their effectiveness in driving tasks remains underexplored. In this work, we bridge this gap by (1) introducing DriveReward, a reasoning trajectory evaluation dataset rigorously labeled via temporally-grounded visual guidance, and augmented with counterfactual driving behaviors., (2) alongside a specialized Vision-Language Reward Model. To address the scarcity of failure cases in conventional datasets, we propose a counterfactual data annotation scheme to construct cases encompassing diverse driving styles and erroneous behaviors. Evaluations on our proposed benchmark reveal that even leading open-source and proprietary VLMs fail to excel across all tasks, highlighting significant room for improvement in existing models. Building on these findings, we subsequently tailor a specialized 1B reward model that outperforms larger VLMs on task-specific reward alignment. Finally, we validate our reward model's effectiveness by integrating it into RL finetuning and multi-modal trajectory scoring across multiple baselines, achieving performance comparable to rule-based reward calculations in both open-loop and closed-loop evaluation.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces DriveReward, a specialized Vision-Language Reward Model for autonomous driving, trained on a counterfactual-augmented reasoning trajectory dataset, which outperforms larger VLMs on reward alignment tasks and achieves performance comparable to rule-based methods in RL finetuning and trajectory scoring.
AI.motivation: Current reward models for autonomous driving rely on hand-crafted rule-based objectives or perception ground truth, limiting generalization for data-scaling. While VLMs have been used as reward models in other domains, their effectiveness in driving tasks is underexplored. This work aims to bridge that gap.
AI.method: (1) Constructed DriveReward, a reasoning trajectory evaluation dataset with temporally-grounded visual guidance and counterfactual driving behaviors. (2) Proposed a counterfactual data annotation scheme to address scarcity of failure cases. (3) Tailored a specialized 1B Vision-Language Reward Model trained on this dataset.
AI.result: Leading open-source and proprietary VLMs fail to excel across all driving reward tasks. The specialized 1B reward model outperforms larger VLMs on task-specific reward alignment. When integrated into RL finetuning and multi-modal trajectory scoring, it achieves performance comparable to rule-based reward calculations in both open-loop and closed-loop evaluations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed DriveReward and specialized VLM-based reward model effectively bridge the gap between VLM capabilities and driving reward modeling, demonstrating that a smaller, task-specific model can outperform larger general-purpose VLMs for autonomous driving reward alignment.

title: NGram-MoSE: Efficient Remote Sensing Super-Resolution via N-Gram Context and Mixture-of-Experts
summary: Remote sensing applications for environmental monitoring and disaster management are frequently constrained by a spatial--temporal trade-off: imagery with fine spatial detail is often acquired less frequently, whereas more temporally available observations are typically coarser. Single-image super-resolution provides a practical means to enhance coarse imagery without changing acquisition schedules, yet many Transformer-based SR models remain computationally expensive and can be sensitive to limited or geographically biased training data, which degrades robustness under out-of-distribution conditions. This paper presents NGram-MoSE, a lightweight Transformer architecture designed to improve both efficiency and texture continuity. NGram-MoSE introduces N-Gram Context Injection to strengthen cross-window local consistency and mitigate window-boundary artifacts, and incorporates a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) feed-forward design to scale capacity through sparse activation without proportional growth in inference cost. Experiments on a geographically disjoint OOD test set show that NGram-MoSE achieves 31.68\,dB PSNR while reducing FLOPs by \(14\times\) relative to a heavyweight Transformer reference. Downstream evaluation on a landslide segmentation benchmark further demonstrates that restoring degraded inputs to the detector training scale improves performance, yielding a 4.47\% absolute gain in mAP@50 over bicubic upsampling, and exhibits stronger cross-scale consistency under scale extrapolation. These results indicate that NGram-MoSE provides an effective SR module for resource-constrained remote sensing pipelines requiring robust generalization.

AI.tldr: NGram-MoSE is a lightweight Transformer for single-image super-resolution in remote sensing that uses N-Gram Context Injection for texture continuity and Mixture-of-Experts for efficient capacity scaling, achieving 31.68 dB PSNR with 14× fewer FLOPs than heavyweight models and improving landslide segmentation mAP@50 by 4.47%.
AI.motivation: Remote sensing faces a spatial-temporal trade-off: fine spatial detail imagery is acquired less frequently than coarser imagery. Existing Transformer-based SR models are computationally expensive and sensitive to biased training data, degrading robustness under out-of-distribution conditions.
AI.method: NGram-MoSE introduces two key components: (1) N-Gram Context Injection to strengthen cross-window local consistency and reduce window-boundary artifacts, and (2) a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) feed-forward design for sparse activation that scales capacity without proportional inference cost growth.
AI.result: Achieves 31.68 dB PSNR on a geographically disjoint OOD test set with 14× FLOPs reduction vs. heavyweight Transformer. In landslide segmentation, yields 4.47% absolute gain in mAP@50 over bicubic upsampling, with stronger cross-scale consistency.
AI.conclusion: NGram-MoSE provides an effective, efficient SR module for resource-constrained remote sensing pipelines requiring robust generalization under out-of-distribution conditions.

title: FiberTune: Preserving Action-Fiber Visual Residuals in Vision-Language-Action Fine-Tuning
summary: Action-supervised fine-tuning of vision-language-action (VLA) policies fits demonstrations effectively but constrains only the directions that change predicted actions, leaving visual structure consistent across action-equivalent states free to collapse. We formalize this as residual visual collapse along local action fibers and propose FiberTune, a training-time objective that preserves teacher-structured visual residuals without adding inference-time overhead. FiberTune uses an online action probe to estimate action-predictive feature directions, filters them from intermediate visual-token representations, and aligns the resulting probe-filtered residuals to a frozen visual teacher while regularizing their effective rank. Under identical training conditions, FiberTune improves over task-loss-only fine-tuning in every one of six controlled simulation settings spanning two benchmarks and two architectures (pi_0.5 and OpenVLA-OFT), as well as on physical SO-101 pick-place; representative gains include +10.7 percentage points SR(5) on long-horizon CALVIN ABC-to-D and physical SO-101 task success rising from 72.7% to 78.1%. Residual diagnostics show that these gains coincide with increased probe-filtered residual teacher alignment and effective rank, consistent with the action-fiber motivation.

AI.tldr: FiberTune prevents visual feature collapse in VLA policy fine-tuning by preserving teacher-structured residuals after removing action-predictive directions, improving success rates across benchmarks and real-world tasks without inference overhead.
AI.motivation: Standard action-supervised fine-tuning of VLA policies causes visual representations to collapse along directions not affecting action prediction (residual visual collapse along "action fibers"), limiting generalization.
AI.method: FiberTune removes action-predictive features (via an online action probe) from intermediate visual tokens, then aligns the remaining residuals to a frozen visual teacher while regularizing effective rank.
AI.result: +10.7 pp SR(5) on CALVIN ABC-to-D, real-world SO-101 success from 72.7% to 78.1%, consistent improvements across 2 benchmarks & 2 architectures (pi_0.5, OpenVLA-OFT).
AI.conclusion: FiberTune is a simple, inference-free training objective that systematically alleviates visual collapse in VLA fine-tuning, validated by residual diagnostics showing increased teacher alignment and effective rank.

title: WaveDiT: Distribution-Aware Wavelet Flow Matching for Efficient 3D Brain MRI Synthesis
summary: Large and demographically balanced datasets are essential for reliable neuroimaging biomarkers. Full-resolution 3D brain MRI synthesis can support data augmentation in this setting, but existing approaches either incur prohibitive computational cost at volumetric scale or rely on lossy latent compression that may compromise anatomical detail. As a result, practical 3D generative augmentation often requires specialized compute infrastructure. We propose WaveDiT, a conditional flow matching framework operating in the coefficient space of a 3D Haar Discrete Wavelet Transform. The model combines factorized spatio-depth attention with band-wise heteroscedastic uncertainty modeling derived from higher-order wavelet statistics. Predicted log-variance is integrated directly into both the flow objective and conditioning pathway, enabling adaptive precision consistent with the heavy-tailed and input-dependent variance structure of anatomical detail. This formulation supports full-resolution 3D synthesis under practical memory and time constraints on a single modern GPU. Evaluation on a multi-site cohort demonstrates improved alignment between generated and real MRI distributions, together with enhanced downstream brain age prediction and region-level anatomical agreement relative to diffusion, latent, and wavelet-based baselines. Code is available at https://github.com/sisinflab/WaveDiT

AI.tldr: WaveDiT is a conditional flow matching model that operates in 3D Haar wavelet coefficient space, enabling full-resolution brain MRI synthesis on a single GPU with improved anatomical fidelity and downstream task performance over existing methods.
AI.motivation: Large, balanced 3D brain MRI datasets are needed for reliable neuroimaging biomarkers, but existing generative augmentation methods are either computationally prohibitive at full resolution or lose anatomical detail through lossy compression.
AI.method: WaveDiT uses conditional flow matching in 3D Haar Discrete Wavelet Transform coefficient space, combining factorized spatio-depth attention with band-wise heteroscedastic uncertainty modeling from higher-order wavelet statistics; predicted log-variance is integrated into both the flow objective and conditioning pathway.
AI.result: Outperforms diffusion, latent, and wavelet-based baselines on a multi-site cohort in distribution alignment, brain age prediction, and region-level anatomical agreement, all under practical memory/time constraints on a single modern GPU.
AI.conclusion: Wavelet-domain flow matching with adaptive uncertainty modeling enables practical full-resolution 3D MRI synthesis for data augmentation without requiring specialized compute infrastructure.

title: Learning to Solve Generative ODEs Beyond the Linear Span
summary: Diffusion and flow generative models sample by integrating a learned ODE, but high quality still requires many sequential model evaluations. Solver learning reduces this cost by adapting scalar coefficients, timesteps, or both, while keeping the backbone model fixed. In this work, we identify a structural bottleneck in this update family: each step remains span-limited. Since the scalar-coefficient update lies in the span of buffered velocity evaluations, it can fit only the in-span component while leaving any out-of-span residual unreachable by scalar recombination alone. We propose SpanLift, a lightweight neural solver that augments scalar-coefficient updates with a spatial residual operator. SpanLift keeps a fixed base solver as an in-span prior and learns a spatial residual operator over the state and velocity buffer. The operator is trained by endpoint teacher matching, preserves the pretrained backbone, and adds no model NFEs. Empirically, the learned correction transfers across base solvers and is predominantly out-of-span. Across pixel-space diffusion, latent flow matching, and precipitation nowcasting, SpanLift achieves state-of-the-art few-step sampling. With only 3 NFE, it improves CIFAR-10 FID from 8.16 to 5.69 and ImageNet FID from 17.37 to 11.83.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes SpanLift, a lightweight neural solver that improves few-step sampling in diffusion and flow generative models by augmenting scalar-coefficient updates with a spatial residual operator, achieving state-of-the-art results with only 3 NFE.
AI.motivation: Existing scalar-coefficient solver learning methods are bottlenecked because each step is span-limited—the update can only fit components within the span of buffered velocity evaluations, leaving out-of-span residuals unreachable by scalar recombination alone.
AI.method: SpanLift augments a fixed base solver (in-span prior) with a lightweight spatial residual operator learned via endpoint teacher matching, operating over the state and velocity buffer without adding model NFEs.
AI.result: Achieves SOTA few-step sampling: CIFAR-10 FID from 8.16→5.69, ImageNet FID from 17.37→11.83 at 3 NFE. The learned correction transfers across base solvers and is predominantly out-of-span.
AI.conclusion: Adding a spatial residual operator to scalar-coefficient updates effectively overcomes the span-limitation bottleneck, enabling high-quality few-step generation while preserving pretrained backbones.

title: BioVid: Autoregressive Video Generation with Biological Behavior Semantic Comprehension
summary: Existing video generation frameworks treat sequence duration as an externally prescribed parameter -- fixed frame counts or text prompts -- producing clips whose temporal boundaries are decoupled from the statistical structure of real behavioral data. This assumption is fundamentally misaligned with biological behavior, where action duration varies naturally across individuals and instances and is encoded in the data itself. We present BioVid, a data-driven autoregressive video generation framework that learns the temporal structure of biological behaviors directly from training data, including their natural length distributions. In the first stage, a Finite Scalar Quantization GAN (FSQ-R3GAN) tokenizer encodes each video frame into a compact discrete representation, combining the stabilized relativistic training objective of R3GAN with FSQ's guaranteed codebook utilization to achieve high-fidelity spatial reconstruction without codebook collapse. In the second stage, a causal Transformer models the resulting token sequences autoregressively and learns to emit an End-of-Sequence (EOS) token when the behavioral event reaches semantic closure, with the termination distribution emerging naturally from the training data rather than any human-specified constraint. Experiments on a human drinking behavior dataset (NTU RGB+D, A001, n=94) demonstrate that BioVid's generated length distribution closely matches that of held-out test data, achieving a Wasserstein-1 distance of 1.24 against the ground truth -- compared to 6.05 for a fixed-length baseline and 15.48 for VideoGPT -- while maintaining competitive spatial fidelity.

AI.tldr: BioVid is a data-driven autoregressive video generation framework that learns temporal structure and natural length distributions of biological behaviors from training data, outperforming fixed-length baselines in matching duration distributions on human drinking behavior data.
AI.motivation: Existing video generation frameworks treat sequence duration as an externally prescribed parameter (fixed frame counts or text prompts), which is misaligned with biological behavior where action duration varies naturally across individuals and is encoded in the data itself.
AI.method: Two-stage framework: (1) FSQ-R3GAN tokenizer encodes video frames into compact discrete representations with high-fidelity spatial reconstruction; (2) A causal Transformer models token sequences autoregressively and learns to emit an End-of-Sequence (EOS) token when the behavioral event reaches semantic closure, with termination distribution emerging naturally from training data.
AI.result: On NTU RGB+D (A001, n=94) human drinking dataset, BioVid achieves Wasserstein-1 distance of 1.24 vs ground truth, compared to 6.05 for fixed-length baseline and 15.48 for VideoGPT, while maintaining competitive spatial fidelity.
AI.conclusion: Learning temporal duration distributions directly from data enables more realistic video generation of biological behaviors compared to externally prescribed duration constraints.

title: Distortion-Aware PETR for BEV Object Detection with Mixed Pinhole-Fisheye Cameras
summary: Fisheye cameras are widely deployed in autonomous driving perception suites for their low cost and full-coverage field of view (FOV), yet their potential remains underleveraged in 3D object detection. Severe radial distortion challenges most BEV detectors by violating the fundamental assumption of uniform sampling. To bridge this gap, we propose Distortion-Aware PETR (DAPETR), a projection-free detector tailored for mixed pinhole-fisheye camera setups. DAPETR incorporates two key learned-adaptive modules: a unified distortion-aware positional embedding that harmonizes positional encodings for image representations with fisheye geometry, and a bidirectional feature-geometry co-modulation module that mutually adapts image features and 3D positional embeddings. In our experiments on a converted KITTI-360 benchmark, we systematically compare our learned adaptive approach against PETR in polar coordinates (PolarPETR). We find that while both methods improve over the baseline, our learned modules achieve superior performance. Crucially, we uncover a negative interaction when combining both strategies, revealing that learned adaptation and explicit geometric reparameterization can conflict. Our final DAPETR model significantly advances the research and benchmark for fisheye BEV detection, providing critical insights into effective distortion-aware 3D perception design other than image rectification.

AI.tldr: DAPETR is a projection-free 3D object detector for mixed pinhole-fisheye camera setups, using learned-adaptive modules (distortion-aware positional embedding and feature-geometry co-modulation) to handle fisheye distortion, outperforming baselines on KITTI-360 while revealing that learned adaptation and explicit geometric reparameterization can conflict.
AI.motivation: Fisheye cameras are widely used in autonomous driving for their low cost and wide FOV, but their severe radial distortion violates the uniform sampling assumption of most BEV detectors, leaving their potential underleveraged in 3D object detection.
AI.method: Proposes DAPETR with two key modules: (1) unified distortion-aware positional embedding that harmonizes positional encodings with fisheye geometry, and (2) bidirectional feature-geometry co-modulation that mutually adapts image features and 3D positional embeddings, without relying on image rectification.
AI.result: On a converted KITTI-360 benchmark, DAPETR outperforms both the baseline PETR and PolarPETR (polar coordinate variant). The study also reveals a negative interaction when combining learned adaptation with explicit geometric reparameterization.
AI.conclusion: DAPETR advances fisheye BEV detection research and provides insights into effective distortion-aware 3D perception design beyond traditional image rectification approaches.

title: BLUE: Toward Better Language Use in Efficient Vision-Language-Action Models for Autonomous Driving
summary: We present BLUE, a minimal method for better language use in vision-language-action (VLA) models for autonomous driving (AD). Through extensive analysis, we reveal that language matters on only a small fraction of routes, but on those routes it can greatly improve or degrade performance. Generating language at every frame is therefore inefficient, since most computation is spent on frames that do not benefit from language. We further show that pretrained VLA hidden states potentially already encode whether language will benefit a given frame, even though scene complexity and kinematic features alone struggle to predict this. Based on this finding, BLUE trains a lightweight gate on frozen VLA hidden states to decide per frame whether to activate language generation or predict actions directly, without modifying the backbone or requiring additional human annotation. With just a 0.11M-parameter gate, BLUE sets a new state of the art on both benchmarks, achieving 76.2% success rate on Bench2Drive and 36 driving score on Longest6 v2, while delivering 2.54x inference speedup and 8.9% success rate improvement over the backbone. BLUE provides a practical path toward efficient language-augmented AD, showing that VLA models can retain the benefits of language at a fraction of the cost. Our code, data, logs and checkpoints are fully available on https://github.com/George-Ling3/BLUE.

AI.tldr: BLUE is a lightweight gate (0.11M parameters) that selectively activates language generation in VLA models for autonomous driving, achieving SOTA results (76.2% on Bench2Drive, 36 on Longest6 v2) with 2.54x speedup and 8.9% success rate improvement, by using frozen VLA hidden states to decide per-frame whether language helps.
AI.motivation: Language benefits only a small fraction of driving routes but can greatly impact performance. Generating language at every frame is computationally wasteful, and existing methods lack selective language activation.
AI.method: BLUE trains a lightweight gate (0.11M parameters) on frozen VLA hidden states to decide per frame whether to activate language generation or directly predict actions, without modifying the backbone or needing additional annotations.
AI.result: Achieves 76.2% success rate on Bench2Drive and 36 driving score on Longest6 v2, with 2.54x inference speedup and 8.9% success rate improvement over the backbone.
AI.conclusion: BLUE provides a practical, efficient path toward language-augmented autonomous driving, showing VLA models can retain language benefits at a fraction of the computational cost.

title: Shift-Dependent Asymmetry: Orthogonal Inverse Low-Rank Adaptation for Federated Medical Segmentation
summary: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) enables efficient federated fine-tuning of segmentation foundation models for medical imaging. However, most federated LoRA methods adopt a uniform aggregation rule, which breaks under the encoder-decoder asymmetry in medical segmentation: the encoder is dominated by appearance shifts, while the decoder is dominated by supervision variations. This mismatch entangles shared anatomy with site-specific biases and harms generalization. To address this, we propose Inverse Asymmetric Tuning (IAT). IAT aligns adaptation with heterogeneity sources by personalizing module-specific components in the encoder to absorb appearance shifts and in the decoder to accommodate site-dependent supervision, while retaining a shared pathway for transferable consensus. However, structural separation alone is insufficient under LoRA's bilinear parameterization, where multiplicative coupling can still cause site-specific updates to leak into the shared direction. We therefore introduce a Subspace Orthogonality Regularizer that penalizes shared-local collinearity in the effective update space, mitigating leakage without extra communication. Experiments show consistent improvements over strong federated LoRA and parameter-efficient FL baselines.

AI.tldr: IAT proposes personalized LoRA modules for encoder (appearance) and decoder (supervision) with a subspace orthogonality regularizer to prevent information leakage, improving federated medical image segmentation.
AI.motivation: Federated LoRA methods use uniform aggregation that fails under encoder-decoder asymmetry in medical segmentation: encoders handle appearance shifts, decoders handle supervision variations, causing entanglement of anatomy with site-specific biases.
AI.method: IAT personalizes module-specific LoRA components in encoder (appearance) and decoder (supervision) with a shared pathway, plus a Subspace Orthogonality Regularizer to penalize shared-local collinearity and prevent site-specific leakage.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over strong federated LoRA and parameter-efficient FL baselines in medical image segmentation tasks.
AI.conclusion: Structural separation with subspace orthogonality regularization effectively handles heterogeneous federated medical segmentation under LoRA's bilinear parameterization.

title: PRPO: Perception-Reinforced Policy Optimization via Token-Level Dynamic Advantage Reshaping
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has become an effective paradigm for improving the reasoning capability of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs). However, existing RLVR methods primarily rely on trajectory-level outcome rewards, which assign identical learning signals across all generated tokens. This coarse-grained credit assignment is fundamentally mismatched to multimodal reasoning, where only a sparse subset of tokens is causally grounded in visual evidence. Consequently, these pivotal perceptual tokens receive weak supervision and are often overwhelmed by language priors or reasoning-template tokens. To address this limitation, we propose Perception-Reinforced Policy Optimization (PRPO), a token-level reinforcement learning framework that explicitly identifies and reinforces pivotal perceptual tokens within long-horizon multimodal reasoning trajectories. PRPO introduces Robust Visual Dependency (RVD), a principled metric that identifies tokens whose predictions are both visually grounded and perturbation-stable, filtering out brittle or noisy visual tokens. Based on RVD, we further propose Perceptual Advantage Reshaping (PAR), a token-level credit assignment technique that amplifies perceptually informative tokens while preserving stable gradients for non-perceptual tokens. Extensive experiments on seven multimodal reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that PRPO consistently outperforms strong LVLM baselines across both 3B and 7B model scales, achieving average gains of 23.3% and 21.1%, respectively. PRPO achieves state-of-the-art performance with improved training efficiency and stronger cross-task generalization. Our findings highlight the importance of fine-grained credit assignment for scalable multimodal reinforcement learning.

AI.tldr: PRPO is a token-level RL framework that identifies and reinforces perceptually important tokens in multimodal reasoning, outperforming baselines by 23.3% (3B) and 21.1% (7B) on average across seven benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing RLVR methods use coarse-grained trajectory-level rewards that assign identical signals to all tokens, failing to properly supervise the sparse subset of causally grounded perceptual tokens in multimodal reasoning.
AI.method: PRPO introduces Robust Visual Dependency (RVD) to identify visually grounded, perturbation-stable tokens, and Perceptual Advantage Reshaping (PAR) for token-level credit assignment that amplifies perceptual tokens while maintaining stable gradients for others.
AI.result: PRPO achieves state-of-the-art performance on seven multimodal reasoning benchmarks, with average gains of 23.3% (3B) and 21.1% (7B) over strong LVLM baselines, with improved training efficiency and cross-task generalization.
AI.conclusion: Fine-grained credit assignment is crucial for scalable multimodal reinforcement learning, and identifying perceptually pivotal tokens significantly improves reasoning capability.

title: Thinking Without Images: Internalizing Visual Manipulation with On-Policy Self-Distillation
summary: ''Thinking with Images'' has emerged as an effective paradigm for fine-grained visual reasoning: by explicitly zooming into relevant regions and reasoning over crops, models can access local evidence that is difficult to recover from a single global image. However, this benefit comes with redundant tool invocations and longer inference traces. Moreover, when such behaviors are learned mainly from outcome reward, the resulting intermediate crops or visual cues can be noisy or fail to faithfully capture task-relevant visual evidence. In this work, we ask whether the reasoning benefits of ''Thinking with Images'' can be internalized through Thinking with Imagination: an internal process that decides where to look and imagines what visual cues closer inspection would reveal without actually invoking tools. We propose Imagine-OPD, an on-policy self-distillation framework in which a teacher plays the role of a ''Thinking with Images'' reasoner during training: it receives privileged zoomed evidence views derived from annotated regions, and supervises the model's own imagination reasoning trajectories. Imagine-OPD does not require an external teacher or high-quality imagination demonstrations. Experiments on vision-centric benchmarks show that Imagine-OPD achieves the best average performance among compared models while significantly reducing inference overhead compared with ''Thinking with Images'' methods.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Imagine-OPD, an on-policy self-distillation framework where a teacher model uses actual zoomed-in visual evidence to supervise a student model's internal "imagined" visual reasoning process, achieving competitive accuracy with lower inference cost compared to explicit "Thinking with Images" methods.
AI.motivation: Current "Thinking with Images" methods improve fine-grained visual reasoning by zooming into image regions, but incur high inference costs (redundant tool invocations, longer traces) and often produce noisy intermediate crops. The authors ask whether these benefits can be internalized through imagination rather than actual tool use.
AI.method: Imagine-OPD: An on-policy self-distillation framework. During training, a teacher model receives privileged access to annotated zoomed-in region evidence (acting as "Thinking with Images"). It supervises the student model to learn its own "imagination" reasoning trajectories — internal decisions about where to look and what visual cues would be found — without requiring actual tool invocation at inference time.
AI.result: Imagine-OPD achieves the best average performance among compared models on vision-centric benchmarks, while significantly reducing inference overhead compared to explicit "Thinking with Images" methods. No external teacher or high-quality imagination demonstrations are required.
AI.conclusion: The reasoning benefits of explicit visual zooming ("Thinking with Images") can be effectively internalized via imagination-based training. The self-distillation setup (teacher with privileged annotations supervising student imagination) is effective without external supervision, offering a practical trade-off between accuracy and efficiency.

title: AUCp: Pseudo-AUC for Inference Model Selection with Unlabeled Validation Data in Abnormality Detection
summary: Abnormality detection is a crucial yet challenging task in medical image analysis. Distinguishing abnormalities from normal data by learning to reconstruct normal-only data alleviates the reliance on labeled datasets. However, many studies, even if unsupervised, rely on a labeled validation set to select the best model for inference from multiple training iterations. For many diseases labeled data are unavailable and substantially time consuming to obtain. To address this, AUCp - a novel metric that supports abnormality detection for unsupervised and self-supervised methods is proposed. Instead of evaluating the realism of reconstructed images to select the best of model for inference, it focuses on actual detection performance and without requiring an annotated test set. Assuming the pseudo ground truth of all unannotated samples in the test set as abnormal/positive and using traditional AUC calculation, AUCp scores are derived. Given a large and representative training set of normal samples, we show mathematical and empirical evidence that model selection using AUCp scores improves disease detection in terms of unsupervised and self-supervised methods over conventional metrics. Using two unsupervised methods for neurologic disease detection and self-supervised methods on diverse datasets, our results demonstrate that the AUCp score effectively identifies the optimal model for inference, significantly enhancing abnormality and disease detection. The corresponding implementations are available in https://github.com/mahfuzmohammad/AUCp.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes AUCp, a novel metric for unsupervised/self-supervised abnormality detection that selects the best model without requiring labeled validation data, by assuming all test samples as pseudo-positive and using traditional AUC calculation.
AI.motivation: Existing unsupervised abnormality detection methods often rely on labeled validation sets for model selection, but labeled data is frequently unavailable and time-consuming to obtain. This creates a practical gap between unsupervised training and supervised model selection.
AI.method: AUCp treats all unannotated test samples as pseudo-positive (abnormal) and uses standard AUC calculation for model selection, assuming a large and representative normal training set. Mathematical and empirical validation is provided.
AI.result: AUCp outperforms conventional metrics (e.g., reconstruction realism) for model selection in both unsupervised and self-supervised abnormality detection across neurologic disease detection and diverse datasets.
AI.conclusion: AUCp is an effective, label-free metric for selecting the optimal model for inference in abnormality detection tasks, eliminating the need for annotated validation sets.

title: MB-Loc: Multi-planar Bird's-eye-view Localization in outdoor LiDAR scenes
summary: Global LiDAR localization is a fundamental task for autonomous navigation systems. Recent methods perform Scene Coordinate Regression (SCR) and achieve superior accuracy over Absolute Pose Regression (APR) solutions by predicting dense 3D world coordinates. However, SCR approaches introduce two major bottlenecks: severe computational inefficiency from processing raw 3D geometries and significant performance degradation under varying sensor viewpoints. To address these limitations, we present MB-Loc, a lightweight and viewpoint-robust SCR framework. Instead of relying on heavy 3D convolutions, we project the input LiDAR scan into a 2.5D Multi-planar Bird's-Eye View (BEV) representation. By slicing the point-cloud along the Z-axis and mapping signed depths into discrete 2D planes, MB-Loc retains essential 3D geometric structures while exploiting the computational tractability of standard 2D CNNs. To handle the inherent sparsity of outdoor LiDAR, we introduce a KL-regularized latent bottleneck that explicitly models spatial uncertainty without injecting stochastic noise. Finally, to ensure rotation robustness, we apply 3D spatial augmentations prior to planar projection, forcing the network to implicitly learn viewpoint-invariant features. We perform extensive experiments on the publicly available NCLT dataset and demonstrate that our proposed method outperforms the current state-of-the-art. Operating at real-time inference speeds, MB-Loc significantly outperforms traditional 3D-SCR architectures in computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: MB-Loc is a lightweight LiDAR localization framework that projects 3D point clouds into 2.5D multi-planar BEV representations, uses KL-regularized latent bottlenecks for spatial uncertainty, and applies 3D augmentations for viewpoint robustness, achieving real-time state-of-the-art performance on the NCLT dataset.
AI.motivation: Existing SCR-based LiDAR localization methods suffer from computational inefficiency due to heavy 3D convolutions on raw point clouds, and performance degrades significantly under varying sensor viewpoints.
AI.method: (1) Projects LiDAR scans into 2.5D multi-planar BEV representation by slicing along Z-axis; (2) Uses standard 2D CNNs instead of heavy 3D convolutions; (3) Introduces KL-regularized latent bottleneck for spatial uncertainty modeling; (4) Applies 3D spatial augmentations before projection for rotation robustness.
AI.result: Outperforms current state-of-the-art on NCLT dataset, operates at real-time inference speeds, and significantly surpasses traditional 3D-SCR architectures in computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The 2.5D BEV projection combined with KL-regularized uncertainty modeling and viewpoint augmentation effectively addresses the efficiency and robustness bottlenecks of 3D-SCR methods.

title: Stain-Aware Wavelet Regularization for Instant Adversarial Purification in Histopathology
summary: Deep learning has become prevalent in computational pathology pipelines that support tasks such as cancer screening and digital pathology analysis. However, the susceptibility of neural networks to adversarial perturbations raises safety concerns for reliable deployment in clinical practice. In histopathological images, this challenge is exacerbated by the difficulty of distinguishing high-frequency adversarial noise from subtle and diagnostically relevant tissue structures. To address this issue, we propose Stain-Aware Wavelet Regularization (SAWR), an adversarial purification framework that leverages multi-level wavelet-domain regularization based on Haar transform to hierarchically disentangle adversarial perturbations from diagnostic structural information. This spectral constraint is further extended to individual histological channels, enabling stain-specific frequency regulation consistent with the biological properties of Hematoxylin and Eosin. When integrated into an instant purification framework, SAWR improves adversarial robustness by up to 10.69\% over the baseline approach, while maintaining texture and spectral fidelity under adversarial perturbations.

AI.tldr: SAWR uses Haar wavelet transform and stain-aware regularization to purify adversarial perturbations from histopathology images, improving robustness by up to 10.69% while preserving diagnostic tissue structures.
AI.motivation: Neural networks in computational pathology are vulnerable to adversarial attacks, and histopathological images are especially challenging because high-frequency adversarial noise can be easily confused with subtle, diagnostically relevant tissue structures.
AI.method: SAWR applies multi-level wavelet-domain regularization via Haar transform to hierarchically separate adversarial noise from diagnostic information, then extends this spectral constraint to individual H&E stain channels for stain-specific frequency regulation.
AI.result: SAWR improves adversarial robustness by up to 10.69% over the baseline while maintaining texture and spectral fidelity under adversarial perturbations.
AI.conclusion: Stain-aware wavelet regularization is an effective adversarial purification approach for histopathology, balancing robustness with preservation of diagnostically relevant image features.

title: Less Is More: Training-Free Acceleration Framework of 3D Diffusion Models for Low-Count PET Denoising via Global-Local Trajectory Reduction
summary: Accurate quantification and uptake measurement in PET are critical for assessing disease progression and supporting clinical decision-making. While high-count PET provides reliable image quality, the associated radiation dose and prolonged acquisition remain significant clinical concerns, motivating the adoption of low-count protocols. Diffusion-model-based methods have demonstrated strong potential for restoring low-count PET to near high-count quality, but their iterative sampling procedure becomes prohibitively expensive when applied to high-resolution 3D PET volumes, introducing substantial inference latency that limits practical clinical deployment. To address these challenges, we propose a training-free Global-Local Skipping Strategy that accelerates diffusion model-based 3D PET denoising while simultaneously improving reconstruction quality. The proposed method is plug-and-play and directly applicable to pre-trained diffusion models without retraining or architectural modification. Specifically, we introduce: (i) a global denoising step skipping strategy that initializes the reverse diffusion process from an intermediate denoising step using a noise-consistent transformation of the low-count input, substantially reducing the number of required denoising steps; and (ii) a local feature reuse shortcut that reuses slowly-varying high-level U-Net features across neighboring denoising steps, further reducing per-step computation while preserving image fidelity. We evaluate the proposed approach on multiple PET tracers from in-house and public datasets, including 18F-FDG PET, 68Ga-DOTATATE PET, and 18F-PSMA PET, demonstrating consistent acceleration of over an order of magnitude alongside improved or comparable reconstruction performance relative to the full-step baseline. Blinded reader studies further confirm enhanced clinical confidence and perceived diagnostic quality.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a training-free Global-Local Skipping Strategy to accelerate diffusion model-based 3D PET denoising for low-count PET reconstruction. It uses global denoising step skipping (starting from an intermediate step) and local feature reuse shortcuts across neighboring steps, achieving >10x speedup with improved or comparable quality across multiple PET tracers.
AI.motivation: Low-count PET protocols reduce radiation dose and acquisition time, but restoration to high-count quality is challenging. Diffusion models show promise but are computationally prohibitive for 3D volumes due to iterative sampling, limiting clinical deployment.
AI.method: A training-free plug-and-play Global-Local Skipping Strategy with two components: (i) global denoising step skipping — initializing from an intermediate step via noise-consistent transformation of low-count input; (ii) local feature reuse shortcut — reusing slowly-varying high-level U-Net features across neighboring steps to reduce per-step computation.
AI.result: Evaluated on 18F-FDG, 68Ga-DOTATATE, and 18F-PSMA PET datasets, achieving over an order of magnitude acceleration with improved or comparable reconstruction quality versus full-step baseline. Blinded reader studies confirmed enhanced clinical confidence and diagnostic quality.
AI.conclusion: The proposed acceleration strategy enables practical clinical deployment of diffusion-based low-count PET denoising without retraining, reducing inference latency while maintaining or improving image quality across multiple tracers.

title: Beyond Consistency: Preserving Temporal Structure in Zero-Shot Video Editing
summary: Existing zero-shot video editing methods rely on pre-trained diffusion models, successfully achieving spatial control and basic temporal consistency but fundamentally fail to preserve the video's original temporal structure.This distinction is critical: temporal consistency ensures visual smoothness, but temporal structure dictates the video's high-level narrative, rhythm, and semantic flow. Without this preservation, the edited output, especially for long videos with complex semantic variations, becomes narratively incoherent and semantically ambiguous. To address this limitation, we introduce a novel zero-shot editing approach that, for the first time, explicitly focuses on preserving the source video's temporal structure. We achieve this by adaptively partitioning the video into semantically distinct clips based on feature similarity and selecting a representative anchor frame for each clip. To enhance both intra-clip fidelity and computational efficiency, we design a clip-adaptive token merging strategy which leverages the anchor's semantic dominance to stabilize the editing. Furthermore, we employ an alternating combination strategy that ensures seamless inter-clip transitions while maintaining semantic distinction. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art results, successfully balancing the preservation of original temporal structure with computational efficiency, and setting a new benchmark for zero-shot video editing fidelity.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a zero-shot video editing method that preserves the source video's temporal structure (narrative/rhythm/semantic flow) by adaptively partitioning videos into semantically distinct clips, using anchor frames and clip-adaptive token merging, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Existing zero-shot video editing methods achieve spatial control and basic temporal consistency but fail to preserve the video's original temporal structure (high-level narrative, rhythm, semantic flow), leading to narratively incoherent and semantically ambiguous outputs, especially for long videos with complex semantic variations.
AI.method: (1) Adaptive partitioning of videos into semantically distinct clips based on feature similarity. (2) Selection of a representative anchor frame for each clip. (3) Clip-adaptive token merging strategy leveraging anchor's semantic dominance for intra-clip fidelity and efficiency. (4) Alternating combination strategy for seamless inter-clip transitions while maintaining semantic distinction.
AI.result: State-of-the-art results, successfully balancing preservation of original temporal structure with computational efficiency, setting a new benchmark for zero-shot video editing fidelity.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method effectively addresses the critical limitation of preserving temporal structure in zero-shot video editing, achieving superior fidelity and coherence compared to existing approaches.

title: DeepMine-Mamba: Mitigating Information Dilution in Mamba-Based State Space Models for Document Image Binarization
summary: Document image binarization aims to separate foreground text from degraded backgrounds while preserving thin, broken, and low-contrast strokes. Although deep learning methods have improved binarization performance, most existing approaches rely on convolutional, transformer-based, or generative architectures, while Mamba-based state space models remain largely unexplored for this task. In this work, we investigate Mamba-based feature propagation and observe that direct state-space propagation may dilute weak foreground cues during long-range modeling, especially faint ink traces, fragmented characters, and boundary-sensitive stroke details. To address this problem, we propose DeepMine-Mamba, a Mamba-based binarization framework equipped with a novel Anti-Dilution Gate that estimates propagation-induced feature changes and selectively restores stroke-sensitive local responses while suppressing unnecessary background enhancement. Experiments on DIBCO/H-DIBCO benchmarks under a strict leave-one-year-out protocol show that DeepMine-Mamba achieves competitive overall performance, with strong average FM and Fps across benchmark years. Ablation results further demonstrate that the Anti-Dilution Gate improves stroke preservation and reduces perceptually significant binarization errors.

AI.tldr: DeepMine-Mamba is a Mamba-based document image binarization framework with an Anti-Dilution Gate that prevents weak foreground cues from being diluted during long-range feature propagation, achieving competitive performance on DIBCO benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing deep learning methods for document binarization use CNNs, transformers, or GANs, but Mamba-based state space models remain unexplored. Direct Mamba propagation can dilute weak foreground cues like faint ink, fragmented characters, and stroke details.
AI.method: Proposes DeepMine-Mamba, a Mamba-based binarization framework with an Anti-Dilution Gate that estimates feature changes from propagation and selectively restores stroke-sensitive local responses while suppressing unnecessary background enhancement.
AI.result: Achieves competitive overall performance on DIBCO/H-DIBCO benchmarks under a strict leave-one-year-out protocol, with strong average FM and Fps scores. Ablations show the Anti-Dilution Gate improves stroke preservation and reduces binarization errors.
AI.conclusion: Mamba-based architectures with anti-dilution mechanisms are effective for document binarization, particularly for preserving weak foreground cues.

title: MaskAlign: Token-Subset Representation Alignment for Efficient Diffusion Training
summary: Representation alignment with pretrained vision models has recently shown strong potential for accelerating diffusion transformer training. By aligning intermediate diffusion features with clean-image representations from self-supervised vision encoders, existing methods improve convergence and generation quality. However, such alignment also introduces a non-trivial constraint: diffusion models operate on noisy inputs whose usable information varies across timesteps, while the reference features are extracted from clean images. In this paper, we revisit this mismatch from a token-level perspective. We find that, under full-token representation alignment, tokens with large alignment-gradient norms exhibit a stable spatial preference, suggesting that the alignment objective does not affect all tokens uniformly and may encourage the model to rely on the complete set of clean-image tokens. To address this issue, we propose MaskAlign, a token-subset representation alignment method that applies alignment to randomly sampled token subsets during training. By exposing the model to different token subsets across iterations, MaskAlign reduces the dependence of representation alignment on the complete token set and encourages alignment behavior that is more stable under token-subset perturbations. To mitigate the information loss caused by directly dropping tokens, we further introduce a lightweight pre-mask token mixing block that shares information across tokens before masking.

AI.tldr: MaskAlign improves diffusion transformer training by randomly aligning only subsets of tokens with clean-image features, reducing dependence on complete token sets while using a pre-mask mixing block to compensate for information loss.
AI.motivation: Existing representation alignment methods between noisy diffusion features and clean-image features suffer from a mismatch due to varying noise levels across timesteps. The authors discover that full-token alignment creates uneven gradient norms with stable spatial preferences, indicating the model becomes overly reliant on complete clean-image token sets.
AI.method: MaskAlign applies representation alignment only on randomly sampled token subsets during training, breaking the dependence on complete token sets. A lightweight pre-mask token mixing block is added before masking to share information across tokens and mitigate information loss from dropped tokens.
AI.result: The token-level analysis reveals that full-token alignment produces uneven gradient norms across tokens with consistent spatial patterns. MaskAlign reduces reliance on complete token sets and achieves more stable alignment behavior under token-subset perturbations.
AI.conclusion: Token-subset alignment (MaskAlign) with pre-mask mixing offers an effective alternative to full-token alignment for better diffusion transformer training.

title: PairWise Image Finder: An Open-source Tool for Finding Visually Aligned Street-Level Image Pairs for Urban Perception Studies
comment: 6 pages, two figures, github repo link near the end
summary: Change detection and scene recognition techniques have been widely applied to Street View Imagery (SVI) to understand changes in scenes across the years. However, metadata alone is often insufficient to reliably find visually aligned image pairs. This study introduces the PairWise image finder, a tool that integrates feature detection and matching, supported by semantic segmentation masks to quantify the visual alignment of two images of varying time periods. The tool outputs the share of matched key features, the matched feature distance and coverage, and the alignment of semantic masks, which enables the user to filter image pairs depending on the alignment quality and use case. The visually aligned pairs derived from the tool can be used to accurately study explicit longitudinal change and help reduce manual effort for perception studies. The usability of the tool is demonstrated through a comparison of longitudinal changes, highlighting the importance of perspective when quantifying changes. The proposed method provides a scalable and open tool for researchers and stakeholders to find high-quality image pairs for urban analysis, perception and related applications.

AI.tldr: This paper presents PairWise image finder, a tool that integrates feature detection, matching, and semantic segmentation to find visually aligned image pairs from Street View Imagery across different time periods, enabling accurate longitudinal change analysis.
AI.motivation: Metadata alone is insufficient to reliably find visually aligned image pairs from Street View Imagery for change detection and scene recognition across years.
AI.method: The tool integrates feature detection and matching with semantic segmentation masks to quantify visual alignment, outputting share of matched key features, matched feature distance/coverage, and semantic mask alignment.
AI.result: The tool enables users to filter image pairs based on alignment quality and use case, demonstrated through comparison of longitudinal changes highlighting the importance of perspective.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method provides a scalable and open tool for researchers to find high-quality image pairs for urban analysis, perception, and related applications, reducing manual effort.

title: Classifying galaxies in the Galaxy10 DECals dataset using Inception and Residual CNNs
comment: 4 pages, 3 figures, 2 tables, published in Proceedings of the 42nd Samahang Pisika ng Pilipinas Physics Conference (SPP 2024)
summary: Image data regarding galactic morphology is expected to increase both in quantity and quality for the next foreseeable years; thus it is important to explore which deep learning architectures adapted for image classification tasks are cost-effective. Residual and Inception networks are ideal for exploring classification convolutional neural networks (CNNs) due to their computational efficiency, achieved through techniques such as residual connections and parallelized inception modules, enabling deeper networks without excessively increasing computational complexity. In this work, we analyze the performance of ResNet101 and InceptionV4 on a spatially-augmented Galaxy10 DECals dataset. Retaining the ten-class classification of galaxies, we modify the image count of each class. We find that ResNet101 and InceptionV4 models achieved accuracies of $\sim$ 90%, comparable with reported performance in the literature. In terms of performance metrics, ResNet101 is superior to InceptionV4. Our results indicate that either of these CNN architectures could serve as a robust foundation for specialized pipelines for classification of galaxy images from upcoming surveys.

AI.tldr: This paper compares ResNet101 and InceptionV4 on the Galaxy10 DECals dataset, finding both achieve ~90% accuracy with ResNet101 slightly outperforming InceptionV4, suggesting both are cost-effective foundations for galaxy classification pipelines.
AI.motivation: With the expected increase in quantity and quality of galactic morphology image data, it is important to explore which deep learning architectures are cost-effective for classification tasks.
AI.method: The authors analyze ResNet101 and InceptionV4 on a spatially-augmented Galaxy10 DECals dataset, retaining ten-class classification while modifying image counts per class.
AI.result: Both models achieved ~90% accuracy; ResNet101 outperformed InceptionV4 across performance metrics.
AI.conclusion: Either CNN architecture could serve as a robust foundation for specialized pipelines for galaxy image classification from upcoming surveys.

title: CSFlow: Aligning Flow Matching with Human Contrast Sensitivity
summary: We introduce Contrast Sensitive Flow (CSFlow), a weighting scheme that connects the human eye's Contrast Sensitivity Function (CSF) to the iterative denoising steps of flow matching. Because real-world images concentrate signal at low spatial frequencies, these components reach high signal-to-noise ratio earlier during continuous diffusion than high-frequency components. When generating images with diffusion or flow matching models, this induces a soft autoregressive structure in Fourier space, where coarse image content stabilizes before fine detail. Meanwhile, the human visual system is unequally sensitive to spatial frequencies: very low and very high frequencies require significantly higher contrast to be perceived. We for the first time merge these observations through two contributions: (1) a metric that estimates which frequencies are generated at each reverse flow interval and (2) timestep weights obtained by aligning the frequencies generated at each noise level with human contrast sensitivity. We validate our contributions experimentally showing that these weights can improve generative performance by lowering FID by 4.7%, increasing Inception Score by 2.2% and improving GenEval scores by 2.5% using inference-only timestep modification or short fine-tuning. Qualitatively, we find that our CSFlow weights lead to better visual realism and less cartoonish appearance of generated images.

AI.tldr: CSFlow introduces a weighting scheme that aligns the iterative denoising steps of flow matching with the human Contrast Sensitivity Function (CSF), improving image generation quality by prioritizing frequencies that human vision is most sensitive to at appropriate timesteps.
AI.motivation: Real-world images have uneven frequency distribution (low frequencies dominate early in diffusion), and the human visual system has unequal sensitivity to different spatial frequencies. The paper aims to merge these two observations to improve generative model performance.
AI.method: (1) A metric estimating which frequencies are generated at each reverse flow interval; (2) Timestep weights derived by aligning generated frequencies with human contrast sensitivity (CSF). Applied via inference-only timestep modification or short fine-tuning.
AI.result: FID reduced by 4.7%, Inception Score increased by 2.2%, GenEval scores improved by 2.5%. Qualitatively, images appear more realistic and less cartoonish.
AI.conclusion: Aligning diffusion timesteps with human contrast sensitivity is a simple yet effective way to improve generative quality, suggesting that perceptual priors can guide generative modeling.

title: Geometry-Aware Fisheye-LiDAR Fusion for Robust 3D Object Detection in Low-Overlap Setups
summary: As autonomous systems expand from capital-intensive robotaxis to cost-sensitive logistics, sensor configurations are increasingly optimized for coverage-per-cost. A prevalent sparse-view setup utilizes dual-fisheye cameras with a roof-mounted LiDAR, introducing severe geometric challenges: extreme radial distortion, minimal overlap, and misalignment between spherical projections and rectilinear grids. BEV fusion algorithms typically force image and point cloud modalities into unified Cartesian grids early in the pipeline, causing significant feature distortion and information loss for wide-view fisheye cameras. To address this, we propose a Geometry-Aware Hybrid Fusion (GA-HF) framework that explicitly accounts for fisheye geometry and BEV feature distortion, where fisheye features are lifted into a polar BEV grid via a Distortion-Aware Lift-Splat-Shoot (LSS) module to preserve native angular density, while LiDAR features are processed in native Cartesian space for metric fidelity of bounding box regression. To bridge these heterogeneous streams, we introduce a Dual-Attention Warping Correction module that applies spatial and channel attention to the warped camera features before fusion, explicitly suppressing artifacts in low-quality peripheral regions while enhancing high-quality semantic cues. GA-HF is evaluated on three benchmarks: KITTI-360, Dur360BEV, and Fisheye3DOD datasets. To the best of our knowledge, it is the first approach to explore LiDAR-fisheye camera fusion. On KITTI-360, GA-HF improves NDS by 4.2% over Cartesian baselines; on Dur360BEV, it surpasses both LiDAR-only and BEVFusion, while significantly reducing orientation error despite the geometric distortions; on Fisheye3DOD, it attains the highest detection score among all fusion methods.

AI.tldr: GA-HF is a novel fusion framework for dual-fisheye + LiDAR setups that uses distortion-aware polar BEV grids and dual-attention warping to significantly improve 3D object detection over Cartesian baselines across three benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Autonomous systems are shifting to cost-effective sparse-view sensor setups (dual-fisheye + LiDAR), but existing BEV fusion methods force fisheye images into Cartesian grids early on, causing severe feature distortion and information loss due to extreme radial distortion, minimal overlap, and spherical-to-rectilinear misalignment.
AI.method: GA-HF employs a Distortion-Aware Lift-Splat-Shoot (LSS) module that lifts fisheye features into a polar BEV grid to preserve native angular density, while LiDAR features remain in Cartesian space. A Dual-Attention Warping Correction module applies spatial and channel attention to suppress artifacts in low-quality peripheral regions before fusion.
AI.result: On KITTI-360, GA-HF improves NDS by 4.2% over Cartesian baselines; on Dur360BEV, it surpasses LiDAR-only and BEVFusion with reduced orientation error; on Fisheye3DOD, it achieves the highest detection score among all fusion methods.
AI.conclusion: GA-HF is the first approach to explore LiDAR-fisheye camera fusion, effectively addressing geometric challenges in sparse-view setups and demonstrating consistent improvements across multiple benchmarks.

title: BLM-SGAN: Bidirectional Language Modeling for Semantic-Spatial Text-to-Image Generation
comment: Published in ICACIn 2024. Appears in Advances on Intelligent Computing and Data Science II, Lecture Notes on Data Engineering and Communications Technologies, vol. 254, Springer, 2025
summary: Despite the success of image generation from text descriptions, it still faces challenges that are difficult to overcome in domains such as natural language processing (NLP) and computer vision (CV). Recent advancements in text-to-image (T2I) models, particularly those utilizing generative adversarial networks (GANs), have significantly improved the synthesis of realistic images across various domains.
